confirmed.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_same_page_update — any logged-in user can modify plugin settings and trigger bulk DB operations
confirmed.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber, contributor, etc.) who can access wp-admin
confirmed.data_flow: Attacker logs in as any role → visits any wp-admin page → receives um-admin-nonce via localized JS (class-enqueue.php:501, load_global_scripts()) → POST wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_same_page_update with nonce + cb_func → check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21-28) passes (nonce is valid) → same_page_update_ajax() (class-admin-settings.php:76) has NO current_user_can() guard → (a) cb_func=um_usermeta_fields: update_option('um_usermeta_fields',...) and UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true) at lines 167-171; (b) cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page: bulk INSERT INTO {prefix}um_metadata at lines 224-229; (c) unrecognized cb_func: do_action('um_same_page_update_ajax_action', $cb_func) at line 236 fires registered third-party hooks with attacker-controlled (sanitize_key'd) string

verdict.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber or above). wp_ajax_nopriv_ variant is absent so an active session is required, but no role or capability check is performed beyond that.
verdict.reasoning:
All links in the claimed attack chain hold:

1. REGISTRATION: class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32 registers only wp_ajax_um_same_page_update (no nopriv variant), confirming the action requires authentication but imposes no capability requirement beyond being logged in.

2. NONCE DISTRIBUTION: class-enqueue.php:501,566 — load_global_scripts() is called unconditionally from admin_enqueue_scripts() for every admin page load. It issues wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') to every authenticated user who loads any wp-admin page. Subscribers can reach profile.php and other non-blocked pages, so obtaining the nonce is trivially possible.

3. NONCE CHECK IS NOT AN AUTHZ CONTROL: class-admin-functions.php:21-28 — check_ajax_nonce() only calls wp_verify_nonce() against the 'um-admin-nonce' action. No current_user_can() call is present, either in check_ajax_nonce() or anywhere in same_page_update_ajax() (class-admin-settings.php:76-237).

4. WP-ADMIN BLOCK DOES NOT PROTECT THE AJAX ENDPOINT: um-actions-wpadmin.php:12 — um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() explicitly short-circuits when DOING_AJAX is defined. Even if a subscriber's UM role configuration blocks wp-admin page navigation, the AJAX endpoint is entirely exempt from that block.

5. SINKS ARE REACHED WITHOUT CAPABILITY GATE:
   - cb_func=um_usermeta_fields: update_option('um_usermeta_fields', ...) and UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true) at lines 167-171 — plugin settings are overwritten.
   - cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page: bulk INSERT INTO {prefix}um_metadata at lines 223-228 — DB operations executed with data from wp_usermeta.
   - unrecognized cb_func: do_action('um_same_page_update_ajax_action', $cb_func) at line 236 — attacker-controlled sanitize_key'd string is passed to any registered third-party hooks.

6. NO EFFECTIVE SANITIZER SAVES IT: sanitize_key() on $cb_func constrains which branch executes but does not prevent the legitimate branches from running when called by a subscriber. The SQL in the um_update_metadata_per_page branch uses wpdb->prepare() for pagination values but the IN() clause is built by imploding an option-stored array (not attacker-controlled at that point), so there is no SQL injection here, but the privilege escalation (any subscriber can trigger bulk DB migration and flip plugin settings) stands without any SQL risk needed.

The vulnerability is real. A subscriber-level user can obtain the um-admin-nonce, POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_same_page_update, and (a) overwrite plugin settings including enabling the custom metadata table, (b) trigger bulk metadata migration INSERT operations, and (c) fire registered do_action hooks with a controlled key string.

confirmed.title: Missing authorization check on wp_ajax_um_same_page_update — any authenticated user can trigger privileged DB operations
confirmed.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber-level). The hook is wp_ajax_um_same_page_update (not nopriv), but no current_user_can() check exists — only a nonce check via check_ajax_nonce().
confirmed.data_flow: POST action=um_same_page_update & nonce=<um-admin-nonce> -> same_page_update_ajax() at line 76 -> check_ajax_nonce() verifies nonce but does NOT call current_user_can('manage_options') -> depending on cb_func: (1) cb_func=um_usermeta_fields writes to wp_options (um_usermeta_fields, um_member_directory_own_table) and triggers DB schema creation; (2) cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page bulk-copies all usermeta rows into a custom table; (3) any other cb_func fires the do_action('um_same_page_update_ajax_action') hook which extensions may listen to.

verdict.auth_required: Any user with wp-admin access (not subscriber-level as claimed). The nonce um-admin-nonce is only distributed via wp_localize_script on admin_enqueue_scripts, which only fires when a user can load a wp-admin page. um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() correctly blocks wp-admin page loads for unprivileged roles (it only exempts DOING_AJAX, but AJAX requests don't carry the nonce either unless the user already visited wp-admin). On a default UM install, the practical minimum is any role configured with can_access_wpadmin — typically Editor, Shop Manager, or similar — NOT subscriber. The vulnerability is real but the stated auth level of subscriber is wrong.
verdict.reasoning:
CONFIRMED REAL but with corrected auth level and severity.

Hook: wp_ajax_um_same_page_update registered in class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32 — requires login but no capability check.

Nonce gate only: check_ajax_nonce() at line 77 calls wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce') with no current_user_can() check anywhere in same_page_update_ajax(). This is a broken access control — nonces are CSRF tokens, not authorization tokens.

Why subscriber claim fails: The nonce um-admin-nonce is generated and localized exclusively in load_global_scripts() via wp_localize_script('um_admin_global', 'um_admin_scripts', ...) hooked on admin_enqueue_scripts. This script only loads when a user visits a wp-admin page. um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() in um-actions-wpadmin.php:12 checks `is_admin() && !defined('DOING_AJAX')` and redirects users without can_access_wpadmin. Subscribers cannot visit any wp-admin page to receive the nonce, so they cannot satisfy wp_verify_nonce(). The DOING_AJAX exemption in the block function is irrelevant here because AJAX requests do not trigger admin_enqueue_scripts either.

Actual exploitable auth level: Any role that UM grants can_access_wpadmin (e.g., editor, shop manager, custom roles). These users can visit any wp-admin page, receive the nonce in the JS global, then POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_same_page_update with cb_func=um_usermeta_fields or um_update_metadata_per_page — triggering DB schema creation, bulk wp_options writes, and bulk copy of all usermeta into a custom table without any privilege check.

Impact of the real vuln: An editor or shop manager (non-admin) can force-enable the custom metadata table (update_option um_member_directory_own_table), overwrite um_usermeta_fields, and bulk-migrate all usermeta — privileged DB operations reserved for administrators. This is a genuine broken access control of medium-high impact, but not exploitable by a pure subscriber on a default install.

No effective sanitizer saves the privileged operations: the only gate is the nonce which is already accessible to lower-privilege wp-admin users. The claim's data flow is accurate; only the auth level is overstated.

confirmed.title: Second-order SQL injection via unescaped implode() into IN clause — um_get_metadata branch
confirmed.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user who can trigger the um_same_page_update AJAX action (see missing-auth finding above). The um_usermeta_fields option must first be poisoned with a SQL-injectable string, which is possible if a malicious value reaches the option via the um_usermeta_fields branch (which derives keys from _um_sortby_custom post meta that an admin stores in the form builder, or from any filter hooking um_metadata_same_page_update_ajax).
confirmed.data_flow: POST cb_func=um_get_metadata -> get_option('um_usermeta_fields') retrieves stored array of meta_key strings -> implode("','", $wp_usermeta_option) interpolated directly into: "SELECT COUNT(*) FROM {$wpdb->usermeta} WHERE meta_key IN ('" . implode("','", $wp_usermeta_option) . "')" at line 182 — no wpdb->prepare() wrapping the IN clause. If any element of the stored option array contains a single quote (e.g. stored as foo') OR SLEEP(5)--), it breaks out of the string literal. The same pattern repeats at line 200 inside wpdb->prepare() for the LIMIT placeholders, but the IN list itself is still concatenated raw before prepare() is called.

verdict.auth_required: Administrator (to poison the um_usermeta_fields option via form builder); any authenticated user can reach the sink but cannot control the option contents
verdict.reasoning:
The SQL injection sink at line 182 of class-admin-settings.php is real: implode("','", $wp_usermeta_option) is concatenated directly into a raw SQL string passed to $wpdb->get_var() with no wpdb->prepare() and no esc_sql() on the IN-clause values. If any element of the stored array contains a single quote, it breaks out of the string literal.

However, the second-order chain is broken at the source-controllability step:

SOURCE: The um_usermeta_fields option is written only by the um_usermeta_fields branch (line 167), which derives values from UM()->builtin()->all_user_fields (plugin-internal, admin-configured form field metakeys) and the filter um_metadata_same_page_update_ajax. Neither path is reachable by a low-privilege authenticated user — they require admin-level post meta writes to the form builder (_um_sortby_custom) or code-level filter hooks.

SINK REACHABILITY: The AJAX action is registered only as wp_ajax_um_same_page_update (not nopriv), so a logged-in user is required. The auth check (UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce()) verifies the um-admin-nonce nonce. This nonce is distributed via wp_localize_script in admin_enqueue_scripts with no current_user_can() gate — but the nonce is only injected into wp-admin HTML pages. The wpadmin blocker (um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role) explicitly skips enforcement when DOING_AJAX is true (line 12), so a subscriber can reach the AJAX endpoint directly. However, obtaining the nonce from the HTML requires loading a wp-admin page, which is blocked for non-admin users by the same function (for non-AJAX requests). That said, WordPress nonces are user-session-based and the subscriber could potentially generate or obtain the nonce through other UM admin pages accessible to them, making sink reachability plausible for any authenticated user.

EXPLOIT PATH: Even if an authenticated user reaches the sink, they read back whatever is in the um_usermeta_fields option. Without first poisoning that option (which requires admin access to the form builder), the query runs with clean meta key strings and returns a valid count. The only way to trigger SQL injection is if an admin has previously stored a malicious meta key via the form builder UI — making this an admin-prerequisite second-order injection.

VERDICT: The vulnerability is real in the sense that the SQL concatenation is unsafe and would be exploitable if the option were poisoned. But the corrected auth level for exploitation is Administrator, not "any authenticated user." At admin level, this is a low-severity issue because admins can already execute arbitrary SQL via many other WordPress mechanisms. The severity is downgraded from medium to low for this reason. No effective sanitizer (esc_sql, wpdb->prepare, intval) is applied to the IN-clause elements, so there is no defense saving the sink — the defense is solely that the source requires admin-level control to corrupt.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_same_page_update allows any logged-in user to trigger privileged DB operations
confirmed.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber/customer level); must be able to visit any wp-admin page to obtain the nonce
confirmed.data_flow: wp_ajax_um_same_page_update hook (class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32) → same_page_update_ajax() (class-admin-settings.php:76). Gate is only UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21), which verifies the 'um-admin-nonce' nonce. That nonce is emitted unconditionally to every logged-in wp-admin visitor via load_global_scripts() (class-enqueue.php:501, called at line 566 for every admin_enqueue_scripts invocation). No current_user_can() check exists anywhere in same_page_update_ajax(). On a valid cb_func of 'um_usermeta_fields' the handler calls update_option('um_usermeta_fields', ...), update_option('um_member_directory_update_meta', ...), UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true), and may invoke UM()->setup()->create_db() (line 220). On 'um_update_metadata_per_page' it reads wp_usermeta, creates the um_metadata table if absent, and bulk-INSERTs all tracked usermeta into it.

verdict.auth_required: Any user with wp-admin page access (editor, shop manager, or any role UM permits to access wp-admin) — NOT bare subscriber/customer. The nonce is distributed via admin_enqueue_scripts which fires on admin page loads; um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role blocks subscribers from those page loads but explicitly bypasses its own check for DOING_AJAX. A subscriber cannot obtain the nonce via a normal AJAX path. Effective floor is a non-admin authenticated user who UM permits wp-admin access.
verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is real but the claimed auth level is overstated. Full chain: (1) wp_ajax_um_same_page_update is a logged-in-only AJAX hook with no current_user_can() check anywhere in same_page_update_ajax(). (2) The sole gate is wp_verify_nonce() against 'um-admin-nonce'. (3) That nonce is emitted unconditionally via load_global_scripts() → wp_localize_script() on every admin_enqueue_scripts invocation, with no capability guard. (4) um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() explicitly skips its redirect when DOING_AJAX is defined (line 12: '! defined("DOING_AJAX")'), so AJAX requests bypass the wpadmin block — but this only matters for the AJAX call itself, not for obtaining the nonce, which requires an actual admin page load where admin_enqueue_scripts fires. (5) Subscribers/customers blocked from wp-admin page loads cannot receive the nonce via the standard path, so the claim that bare subscribers can exploit this is wrong. The real affected tier is authenticated users with any wp-admin page access (editors, shop managers, WooCommerce managers). Such users can call same_page_update_ajax() with cb_func=um_usermeta_fields to: update_option('um_usermeta_fields'), update_option('um_member_directory_update_meta'), UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true), and potentially invoke UM()->setup()->create_db(). With cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page they can trigger bulk INSERT of all wp_usermeta into a custom table. These are administrator-level operations being performed without a manage_options check. No sanitizer saves this — the cb_func is sanitize_key() gated to a fixed string allowlist so injection is not the issue; the issue is pure missing authorization. The vulnerability is real, severity is medium (not high) because it requires at minimum non-admin wp-admin access, the operations are disruptive/data-leaking (DB structure changes, bulk meta exposure) but not direct RCE or credential theft.

confirmed.title: Commented-out per-target capability check enables IDOR for sub-admin editors across all six user-status mutation methods
confirmed.auth_required: Authenticated, edit_users capability (e.g. Editor role granted edit_users)
confirmed.data_flow: class-users-columns.php:387 handle_bulk_actions() receives $userids from WP bulk-action POST -> line 409 array_map('absint', $userids) -> lines 415/434/452/469/487/505 iterate and call UM()->common()->users()->approve/reject/deactivate/reactivate/set_as_pending/send_activation($user_id) -> each delegates to can_be_approved/can_be_rejected/can_be_deactivated/can_be_reactivated/can_be_set_as_pending/can_activation_send in class-users.php -> those methods contain /*if (!$this->can_current_user_edit_user($user_id)) { return false; }*/ COMMENTED OUT at lines 347, 440, 527, 624, 720, 842 -> no check verifies that the acting user outranks or is permitted to modify the target user -> state mutation proceeds for any valid user ID

verdict.auth_required: Authenticated, edit_users capability — Administrator by default; requires explicit capability grant to lower roles for exploitation
verdict.reasoning:
The data flow described is confirmed. handle_bulk_actions() at class-users-columns.php:387 gates on check_admin_referer('um-bulk-users') and current_user_can('edit_users'), then iterates user IDs calling approve/reject/deactivate/reactivate/set_as_pending/send_activation without any per-target rank check. All six can_be_* methods in class-users.php have the per-target check commented out at lines 347, 440, 527, 624, 720, 842.

The real but narrower finding: the commented-out can_current_user_edit_user() would NOT have fixed an admin-vs-admin IDOR either, because it only re-checks the actor's edit_users capability (already confirmed at the outer gate) and never calls current_user_can('edit_user', $target_id) — which is the WordPress core check that enforces role hierarchy. So the commented code was only a partial mitigation anyway.

The actual gap is the absence of current_user_can('edit_user', $target_id) in the per-target path, allowing any edit_users holder to mutate UM membership status for users at equal or higher WP role levels. However, WordPress grants edit_users exclusively to Administrators on a default install. Exploitation requires a non-default configuration where edit_users has been granted to a lower-privileged role (e.g., Editor or custom sub-admin). This makes it a real vulnerability in misconfigured or multi-admin environments, but not exploitable on a default WordPress installation. Severity is low rather than medium because the prerequisite (non-default capability grant) substantially reduces the realistic attack surface. The nonce check also ensures only CSRF-protected authenticated requests reach the sink.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on um_same_page_update_ajax — any logged-in user can invoke privileged admin data operations
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: $_POST['cb_func'] → sanitize_key() → branch dispatch → update_option('um_usermeta_fields'), update_option('um_member_directory_update_meta'), UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true)

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is confirmed real. Full chain:

1. REGISTRATION: class-admin-ajax-hooks.php line 32 registers `wp_ajax_um_same_page_update` (logged-in only, not nopriv) pointing to `same_page_update_ajax()`.

2. NONCE DISTRIBUTION: class-enqueue.php line 501 creates `wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce')` inside `load_global_scripts()`, which is called from `admin_enqueue_scripts`. WordPress fires `admin_enqueue_scripts` for ANY logged-in user who visits wp-admin — including subscribers — so every logged-in user can obtain this nonce simply by visiting /wp-admin/.

3. NONCE-ONLY CHECK: `UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce()` in class-admin-functions.php lines 21-28 calls only `wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce')` and then `wp_send_json_error` on failure. There is NO `current_user_can()` check — not in this wrapper, not in `same_page_update_ajax()`, not in the hook registration.

4. wp-admin BLOCK IS BYPASSED: um-actions-wpadmin.php line 12 shows `um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role()` only fires when `! defined('DOING_AJAX')`. All AJAX requests define `DOING_AJAX`, so the subscriber wp-admin block is intentionally skipped for AJAX — confirming subscribers can call this endpoint regardless of the 'can_access_wpadmin' role setting.

5. SINK: With cb_func=um_usermeta_fields (after sanitize_key), the handler calls update_option('um_usermeta_fields', ...), update_option('um_member_directory_update_meta', time()), and UM()->options()->update('member_directory_own_table', true) — all privileged admin operations.

No effective defense exists: the nonce is not capability-gated; it is distributed to all logged-in users; DOING_AJAX bypasses the wp-admin subscriber block; and the function itself has zero capability checks. The stated data flow, auth level, and severity are all correct.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on um_member_directory_default_filter_settings — any logged-in user can invoke admin filter rendering
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: wp_ajax_um_member_directory_default_filter_settings → default_filter_settings(): only check is UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21-27), which verifies the 'um-admin-nonce' nonce but performs no is_user_logged_in() or current_user_can() check. The nonce itself is emitted to every user who visits any wp-admin page via wp_localize_script in class-enqueue.php:501, called unconditionally on admin_enqueue_scripts at class-enqueue.php:566. $_REQUEST['key'] → sanitize_text_field() → show_filter() which resolves field definitions and renders admin filter HTML. No capability gate anywhere in the call chain.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning:
All four links in the claimed exploit chain verify:

1. REGISTRATION: class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:30 registers the action as wp_ajax_um_member_directory_default_filter_settings (no nopriv variant), so WP core enforces is_user_logged_in() — but nothing beyond that.

2. NONCE DISTRIBUTION: admin/class-enqueue.php:499-521 emits wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') into um_admin_scripts.nonce via wp_localize_script inside load_global_scripts(), which is called unconditionally at line 566 on every admin_enqueue_scripts invocation. In WordPress core, subscribers can load wp-admin/index.php (WP does not block subscriber-role users from the dashboard), so they receive this nonce on every wp-admin page load.

3. NONCE CHECK ONLY, NO CAPABILITY CHECK: class-admin-functions.php:21-27 — check_ajax_nonce() calls wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce') and sends a JSON error on failure. It performs no is_user_logged_in() (redundant given wp_ajax_ hook), no current_user_can(), and no role check. The function is the sole authorization gate for default_filter_settings().

4. HANDLER: class-member-directory.php:3230-3239 — default_filter_settings() calls check_ajax_nonce(), reads $_REQUEST['key'] through sanitize_text_field() (not a security barrier for this vulnerability class), and $_REQUEST['directory_id'] through absint(), then passes both to show_filter() with $admin=true to render admin-side filter HTML. show_filter() at line 797 calls um_can_view_field() which gates on field visibility settings, not on user role for admin rendering — the $admin=true parameter causes it to render admin configuration UI unconditionally for visible fields.

No saving check exists: sanitize_text_field() on $filter_key and absint() on $directory_id are input sanitizers, not access controls. There is no current_user_can('manage_options') or any capability gate anywhere in the call chain.

The exploit path is: subscriber logs in → visits wp-admin/index.php → extracts um_admin_scripts.nonce from page source → POSTs to admin-ajax.php with action=um_member_directory_default_filter_settings&nonce=&key=&directory_id= → receives admin filter configuration HTML. Impact is read-only disclosure of admin filter field definitions and directory configuration, not a write primitive, which justifies medium severity rather than high.

confirmed.title: Missing Authorization Check on AJAX DB-Migration Handler (same_page_update_ajax)
confirmed.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber, customer, etc.)
confirmed.data_flow: wp_ajax_um_same_page_update (class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32) -> same_page_update_ajax() (class-admin-settings.php:76). Handler calls check_ajax_nonce() (verifies 'um-admin-nonce') but has no current_user_can() check. The 'um-admin-nonce' is emitted unconditionally to ALL logged-in users via wp_localize_script('um_admin_global', ...) in load_global_scripts() (class-enqueue.php:501), which is called from admin_enqueue_scripts with no capability guard (class-enqueue.php:566). Attacker visits any wp-admin URL, extracts nonce from page source/JS, then POSTs action=um_same_page_update with cb_func=um_usermeta_fields / um_get_metadata / um_update_metadata_per_page.

verdict.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber or above). The AJAX hook uses wp_ajax_ (not nopriv), so login is required, but no capability check gates the handler.
verdict.reasoning: The attack path is fully confirmed by source code. (1) Hook registration at class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32 uses wp_ajax_um_same_page_update — authenticated users only, no capability gate. (2) The handler same_page_update_ajax() at class-admin-settings.php:76 calls only UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce(), which resolves to wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce') in class-admin-functions.php:21-28 — pure nonce check, no current_user_can() anywhere in the function or its callees. (3) The nonce is emitted unconditionally in load_global_scripts() (class-enqueue.php:501) via wp_localize_script with no capability guard, called from admin_enqueue_scripts() at line 566 with no wrapping current_user_can(). A subscriber visiting wp-admin/profile.php (always accessible to logged-in users in WordPress core) receives um_admin_scripts.nonce in page HTML. (4) um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() (um-actions-wpadmin.php:12) explicitly exempts DOING_AJAX requests, so the AJAX endpoint itself cannot be blocked by the role-based redirect. (5) The three cb_func branches — um_usermeta_fields (writes um_usermeta_fields option, triggers DB migration), um_get_metadata (leaks usermeta count), um_update_metadata_per_page (bulk-inserts into wp_um_metadata) — all execute without any privilege check. The only current_user_can('manage_options') in the file (line 2628) is in the unrelated save_settings_handler() for form POST saves. No sanitizer, ownership check, or WP core defense closes the gap. A subscriber can trigger a full DB migration of all user metadata.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on AJAX dismiss_notice handler — any logged-in user can suppress admin security notices
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: $_POST['key'] → sanitize_key() → dismiss() → $hidden_notices[] → update_option('um_hidden_admin_notices', ...) — permanently hides the keyed admin notice for all admins. The required nonce ('um-admin-nonce') is distributed unconditionally to every logged-in user via wp_localize_script in class-enqueue.php:501, called from admin_enqueue_scripts with no capability gate.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning: The AJAX handler wp_ajax_um_dismiss_notice (class-admin-notices.php:34) is registered only under wp_ajax_ (not nopriv), so WordPress core blocks unauthenticated requests — the auth level of "any logged-in user" is correct. The handler calls check_ajax_nonce() (admin-functions.php:21-28) which only runs wp_verify_nonce() against 'um-admin-nonce'; there is no current_user_can() or capability check anywhere in dismiss_notice() or the downstream dismiss() method. The nonce 'um-admin-nonce' is created and distributed unconditionally in load_global_scripts() (class-enqueue.php:499-522) via wp_localize_script, which is called at the very top of admin_enqueue_scripts() with no capability gate. WordPress fires admin_enqueue_scripts for every logged-in user who accesses any wp-admin URL — including subscribers who have only the 'read' capability and a dashboard landing page. Therefore any subscriber can obtain the nonce and POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_dismiss_notice with a valid key, causing update_option('um_hidden_admin_notices', ...) to permanently suppress admin security notices (e.g., the forms_secure_suspicious_activity warning) site-wide for all administrators. No effective defense exists: the nonce is a CSRF token, not an authorization control. The claimed data flow is accurate and the vulnerability is real.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on force_dismiss_notice admin_init handler — any logged-in user can suppress admin security notices via GET/POST
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: $_REQUEST['um_dismiss_notice'] → sanitize_key() → $hidden_notices[] → update_option('um_hidden_admin_notices', ...) guarded only by wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['um_admin_nonce'], 'um-admin-nonce') with no current_user_can() check. The 'um-admin-nonce' nonce is issued to all logged-in users via wp_localize_script (class-enqueue.php:501) on every admin page load without a capability check.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. Three independent facts confirm the full exploit path holds:

1. HOOK CONTEXT — force_dismiss_notice() is registered on admin_init (class-admin-notices.php:35). WordPress fires admin_init for every authenticated request that reaches wp-admin, including subscribers. There is no is_admin() + current_user_can() guard on the hook registration itself.

2. NONCE DISTRIBUTION WITHOUT CAPABILITY GATE — The 'um-admin-nonce' nonce is created unconditionally in load_global_scripts() (class-enqueue.php:501) via wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') and injected into every wp-admin page load via wp_localize_script. There is no current_user_can() check before this call. Any subscriber who can open any wp-admin URL receives the nonce in the page's inline JavaScript.

3. NO CAPABILITY CHECK IN HANDLER — force_dismiss_notice() (class-admin-notices.php:1111-1126) performs only wp_verify_nonce() and then directly calls update_option('um_hidden_admin_notices', ...). There is no current_user_can() or any other authorization check in the function body.

The render-side check at line 151 (current_user_can('manage_options') inside render_notices()) is purely a display gate for admin notices output — it has no bearing on the write path.

The separate AJAX dismiss_notice handler (line 1083) calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce(), but that resolves to the frontend nonce verifier in class-functions.php:41 which also contains no capability check — confirming the pattern is systemic, not accidental.

Impact is limited to notice suppression: a subscriber can permanently hide security-relevant admin notices from actual administrators by repeatedly appending hidden notice keys to um_hidden_admin_notices. This is not a privilege escalation or data breach, which correctly bounds the severity at medium rather than high/critical. Auth level stated in the claim (subscriber or above) is accurate.

confirmed.title: Broken Access Control on same_page_update_ajax — any logged-in user can trigger admin-only DB operations
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber, customer, etc.)
confirmed.data_flow: 1. AJAX action 'wp_ajax_um_same_page_update' is registered at class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32 — no nopriv variant, so authentication is required, but NO capability check is enforced. 2. The handler calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21-28), which only verifies the nonce 'um-admin-nonce' via wp_verify_nonce() — there is no current_user_can() call anywhere in the handler. 3. The nonce 'um-admin-nonce' is created unconditionally for every logged-in user via wp_localize_script() in class-enqueue.php:501, called from admin_enqueue_scripts() at line 566 with no capability gate — any subscriber visiting any wp-admin page receives the nonce in the JS variable um_admin_scripts.nonce. 4. With that nonce, a subscriber can POST action=um_same_page_update&nonce=<nonce>&cb_func=um_usermeta_fields to rebuild and overwrite the 'um_usermeta_fields' DB option (line 167) and reset the 'um_member_directory_own_table' plugin option (line 171); POST cb_func=um_get_metadata to read sensitive metadata counts (line 179-185); POST cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page&page=1 to trigger a mass INSERT of all usermeta into the custom um_metadata table, including creating that table if absent (lines 193-234). These are destructive, privileged database operations gated only on a nonce.

verdict.auth_required: Any authenticated WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning:
All four links of the exploit chain hold under adversarial review:

1. REGISTRATION (class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32): wp_ajax_um_same_page_update is registered with no nopriv variant, so WordPress enforces authentication but no capability.

2. HANDLER AUTH (class-admin-settings.php:76-77, class-admin-functions.php:21-27): same_page_update_ajax() calls only check_ajax_nonce(), which calls wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce'). There is no current_user_can() call anywhere in the handler or in check_ajax_nonce(). The nonce check is the sole gate.

3. NONCE DELIVERY (class-enqueue.php:494-522, 561-566): load_global_scripts() calls wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') and passes it via wp_localize_script() into the JS variable um_admin_scripts.nonce. This function is called unconditionally at line 566 — before the is_own_screen() capability-gated block — so every logged-in user who visits any wp-admin page (including profile.php or the dashboard) receives the nonce. There is no current_user_can() or capability gate on load_global_scripts().

4. BYPASS OF wp-admin ROLE BLOCK (um-actions-wpadmin.php:12): um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role() skips its redirect when DOING_AJAX is defined. WordPress defines DOING_AJAX for all admin-ajax.php requests. So even if a role is blocked from browsing wp-admin pages, the AJAX endpoint itself is never blocked by this guard. For nonce acquisition: a subscriber visiting any permitted wp-admin page (which WordPress core allows by default for all logged-in users, e.g. profile.php) receives the nonce from load_global_scripts().

5. IMPACT: With a valid nonce, a subscriber can POST cb_func=um_usermeta_fields to overwrite the um_usermeta_fields DB option and reset member_directory_own_table; cb_func=um_get_metadata to read a count of sensitive usermeta rows; cb_func=um_update_metadata_per_page to mass-INSERT all usermeta (including wp_capabilities, WooCommerce order data) into um_metadata, creating the table if absent. These are destructive, privileged DB operations.

No saving defense was found: no current_user_can(), no role check, no ownership check exists in same_page_update_ajax() or check_ajax_nonce(). The nonce string 'um-admin-nonce' is distributed to all logged-in users without capability gating. The vulnerability is real, the stated auth level (any logged-in user) is correct, and the severity is correctly high.

confirmed.title: Broken Access Control on default_filter_settings AJAX — any logged-in user can query user metadata
confirmed.data_flow: Attacker visits /wp-admin/ as any logged-in user → wp_localize_script() bakes um-admin-nonce into um_admin_global JS (class-enqueue.php:501, called unconditionally from admin_enqueue_scripts()) → attacker extracts nonce → POSTs action=um_member_directory_default_filter_settings&nonce=<nonce>&key=<filter_field>&directory_id=<id> → default_filter_settings() calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() which only verifies the nonce (no current_user_can()) → calls show_filter($filter_key, ...) → show_filter() executes $wpdb->get_col("SELECT DISTINCT meta_value FROM wp_usermeta WHERE meta_key = %s") (class-member-directory.php:861-869) and returns distinct user metadata values as rendered HTML in JSON response

verdict.auth_required: Any authenticated user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. All four links in the claimed chain hold:

1. NONCE DISTRIBUTION (confirmed): class-enqueue.php line 566 calls load_global_scripts() unconditionally inside admin_enqueue_scripts() with no capability guard before the call. load_global_scripts() (line 494-522) bakes wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') into um_admin_scripts JS object via wp_localize_script(). Any logged-in user visiting any /wp-admin/ page (including /wp-admin/profile.php, accessible to subscribers) receives this nonce.

2. AJAX REGISTRATION (confirmed): class-admin-ajax-hooks.php line 30 registers the action as wp_ajax_um_member_directory_default_filter_settings (not wp_ajax_nopriv_). WP core blocks unauthenticated users, but all logged-in users can invoke it.

3. MISSING CAPABILITY CHECK (confirmed): check_ajax_nonce() in class-admin-functions.php lines 21-28 calls only wp_verify_nonce() — there is no current_user_can() or similar capability check anywhere in check_ajax_nonce() or in default_filter_settings() itself.

4. SQL SINK (partially confirmed — no SQL injection, but unauthorized data access confirmed): show_filter() uses $wpdb->prepare() with %s placeholder (lines 861-868), so no SQL injection. However, $attrs['metakey'] comes from the registered field definition for the attacker-supplied $filter_key, meaning for any valid registered UM field (role, last_login, custom profile fields), the query runs and returns all distinct meta_value rows from wp_usermeta to the subscriber-level caller. This is real unauthorized metadata enumeration.

What saves it from being HIGH severity: (a) the SQL itself is properly parameterized — no injection path; (b) $attrs['metakey'] comes from a server-side field registry, not directly from user input; (c) um_can_view_field($attrs) is checked (line 797) and may suppress some fields. What makes it genuinely MEDIUM: a subscriber can enumerate all distinct values of any registered UM user meta field (including roles, potentially sensitive profile data) by cycling through known field keys, which is a clear broken access control / information disclosure issue on a default install. The claimed auth level (subscriber+) is correct.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_get_icons — any logged-in user can call admin-only handler
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: class-enqueue.php:501 unconditionally outputs wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') to every wp-admin page load via admin_enqueue_scripts → any logged-in user obtains the nonce. class-forms.php:26 calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() which only calls wp_verify_nonce() with no current_user_can() gate (class-admin-functions.php:21-28). The handler at class-forms.php:25-59 then returns the full icon list from get_option('um_icons_list').

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
verdict.reasoning: The full exploit path holds on all three legs. (1) Hook: wp_ajax_um_get_icons at class-forms.php:19 uses wp_ajax_ (not nopriv), so any logged-in user can trigger it. (2) Nonce acquisition: class-enqueue.php:501 outputs wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') unconditionally into um_admin_scripts.nonce via wp_localize_script inside load_global_scripts(), which is called from admin_enqueue_scripts with no current_user_can() guard anywhere in the file. Subscribers have the 'read' cap and can visit wp-admin/profile.php, which fires admin_enqueue_scripts and injects the nonce into the page. (3) Auth bypass: class-admin-functions.php:21-28 check_ajax_nonce() calls only wp_verify_nonce() — no current_user_can() gate whatsoever. With the nonce in hand from step 2, the subscriber passes the only check. The handler then returns get_option('um_icons_list'), which set_icons_options() populates from FontAwesome's icons.json (CSS class names and labels — non-sensitive public icon library metadata). No saving mechanism is present. Severity is correctly low: the capability check is genuinely missing, auth level is correctly stated as subscriber-or-above, but the disclosed data (FontAwesome icon names already shipped in the plugin's public assets) carries no real confidentiality impact. No sanitizer or WP core defense rescues it — wp_verify_nonce() alone is not a capability check.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_same_page_update — subscriber can rebuild usermeta option, copy all usermeta to custom table, and read usermeta counts
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or above)
confirmed.data_flow: class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:32 registers wp_ajax_um_same_page_update → class-admin-settings.php:76 same_page_update_ajax(). Line 77 calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() — nonce-only, no current_user_can(). Attacker supplies $_POST['cb_func'] (sanitize_key at line 83). Branch 'um_usermeta_fields' (line 85): queries postmeta and usermeta tables, rebuilds the 'um_usermeta_fields' option, calls update_option('member_directory_own_table', true) — altering plugin data structures. Branch 'um_get_metadata' (line 174): returns COUNT(*) of usermeta rows, leaking DB size information. Branch 'um_update_metadata_per_page' (line 186): reads all usermeta rows matching the option list and bulk-inserts them into wp_um_metadata via a raw INSERT (line 224-229) using implode of wpdb->prepare'd values — a low-privilege user can trigger mass data migration. The admin nonce is accessible to any subscriber via class-enqueue.php:501.

verdict.auth_required: WordPress user with wp-admin access (can_access_wpadmin = 1 in UM role config — administrator by default; not subscriber/author/contributor/editor on a default UM install)
verdict.reasoning:
The code defect is real: same_page_update_ajax() at class-admin-settings.php:76 calls only check_ajax_nonce() (nonce-only, no current_user_can()), and wp_ajax_um_same_page_update is registered without any capability guard. However, the claim's stated auth level of "subscriber or above" is wrong because the nonce acquisition step fails for low-privilege users.

The 'um-admin-nonce' nonce is exclusively created inside admin_enqueue_scripts (class-enqueue.php:501 via load_global_scripts()). All other creation points (dragdrop template, modal form templates) are also wp-admin-only. There is no frontend page, REST endpoint, or nopriv AJAX handler that exposes this nonce to non-admin users.

By default, _um_can_access_wpadmin = 0 for subscriber, author, contributor, and editor roles (class-config.php:817,833,849,865). The UM wpadmin block (um-actions-wpadmin.php:9-25) runs at init:99 — before admin_enqueue_scripts — and redirects users without can_access_wpadmin away from wp-admin page loads. The DOING_AJAX exception on line 12 only bypasses the block for admin-ajax.php calls, not for wp-admin page loads that would serve the nonce.

A subscriber hitting admin-ajax.php directly has no way to supply a valid 'um-admin-nonce' nonce because they cannot reach any page that creates one for their session. WordPress nonces are user-session specific (wp_verify_nonce checks the requesting user's session token), so an admin's stolen nonce would not verify for a subscriber's session.

The vulnerability is therefore exploitable only by users who already have can_access_wpadmin = 1 (administrators by default, or custom UM roles where an admin explicitly granted wp-admin access). For those users, the missing capability check in same_page_update_ajax() is real — a user with read-only wp-admin access (but not manage_options) could trigger the usermeta rebuild, metadata migration, and data structure alteration. But on a default UM install this means administrator-only, making the impact trivial (admins have full access anyway).

The SQL in um_get_metadata (line 182) uses an implode of stored option values (not direct $_POST input), so there is no SQLi path from this AJAX handler for a non-admin. The 'um_usermeta_fields' option is only writable via the um_usermeta_fields branch, which itself is gated by the same nonce that only admins can obtain by default.

Severity is downgraded to low: the defect exists but requires wp-admin access to exploit, and on default installs only administrators have that access.

confirmed.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_get_users allows any logged-in user to enumerate all usernames and IDs
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (including Subscriber role)
confirmed.data_flow: 1. Attacker logs in as a low-privilege user (e.g. Subscriber) and visits any wp-admin page (e.g. /wp-admin/). 2. admin_enqueue_scripts fires unconditionally (class-enqueue.php:566 -> load_global_scripts() at line 494), embedding a valid 'um-admin-nonce' into the JS global um_admin_scripts.nonce for every admin page visitor. 3. Attacker POST-requests admin-ajax.php with action=um_get_users&nonce=<obtained_nonce>&page=N. 4. get_users() (class-users.php:19) calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21-27), which only verifies the nonce action 'um-admin-nonce' — no current_user_can() check is ever performed. 5. WP_User_Query runs with fields=['ID','user_login'] (class-users.php:26-40) and returns paginated results covering every user account on the site. Adding avatar=1 to the request appends avatar URLs. Total user count is also returned, enabling full enumeration.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (Subscriber role or above)
verdict.reasoning:
The full exploit chain holds without any effective defense:

1. AJAX hook registration (class-users.php:16): `add_action('wp_ajax_um_get_users', ...)` — `wp_ajax_` prefix requires login but imposes no capability check. No `nopriv` variant exists, so unauthenticated users are blocked, but that is the only gate.

2. Nonce acquisition: `load_global_scripts()` (class-enqueue.php:494-526) calls `wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce')` and stores it in `um_admin_scripts.nonce` unconditionally. This method is called without any capability guard at line 566 from `admin_enqueue_scripts`. WordPress core redirects Subscribers (who hold the `read` capability) to `/wp-admin/profile.php` — a genuine admin page — where `admin_enqueue_scripts` fires and the nonce is injected into the page. A Subscriber simply needs to load `/wp-admin/` once to obtain a valid nonce.

3. Nonce-only "auth" (class-admin-functions.php:21-27): `check_ajax_nonce()` calls `wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce')` and nothing else. There is no `current_user_can()` call anywhere in the call chain — not in `check_ajax_nonce()`, not in `get_users()`, not in any filter on `um_get_users_list_ajax_args`.

4. Data exposure (class-users.php:26-54): `WP_User_Query` runs with `fields => ['ID', 'user_login']`, paginated, covering the entire user table. `get_total()` reveals the full user count. Adding `avatar=1` to the request appends avatar URLs. No `exclude`, `role__in`, or ownership scoping is applied by default.

No effective mitigating control exists: the nonce only proves the requester visited an admin page (which Subscribers can do), not that they hold any administrative capability. The stated severity of medium is appropriate — this is user enumeration (IDs, logins, avatars), not direct privilege escalation or data modification. The auth_required level of "any logged-in user including Subscriber" is precisely correct.

confirmed.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated image uploads (nopriv AJAX)
confirmed.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] are read, but the rejection branch at line 519 is `if ( !wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` — the && means the check only fires when the user IS logged in; for the wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload path is_user_logged_in() is always false so the nonce is never verified. Control then reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_image() which writes to the publicly-accessible temp directory (wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/).

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The && logic flaw at line 519 is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` never fires for unauthenticated requests because is_user_logged_in() is always false on the nopriv path, making the nonce check completely inert for those requests.

The full exploit path to upload_image() at line 638 is clear for an unauthenticated attacker using user_id=empty and set_mode=register: (1) $user_id=null bypasses the line-531 login gate since it tests `$user_id &&`; (2) mode=register bypasses the line-542 profile-mode block; (3) null $user_id bypasses the line-547 register+user_id block; (4) the additional per-field permission checks at lines 612-631 are wrapped in `if ('profile' === $mode)` and are entirely skipped for register mode; (5) a valid published um_form post ID is required but present by default on any Ultimate Member install; (6) a valid field key from that form is required but is rendered in page HTML.

The one partial mitigation that prevents the worst outcome: wp_handle_upload() is called with MIME type validation enforced (JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF whitelist by default), which prevents PHP webshell upload. This limits impact to unauthenticated arbitrary image file write to wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ — a publicly-accessible directory with no .htaccess protection found in the codebase.

The rate limiting (is_rate_limited at line 459) is opt-in and disabled by default — it explicitly returns false when the ajax_nopriv_rate_limit option is not set (line 63 of class-functions.php).

Severity of medium is appropriate given that arbitrary code execution is prevented by the MIME check, but unauthenticated image writes to a public directory enable disk exhaustion, potential EXIF-based information disclosure, and server-side image processing abuse. Auth level of none is correct.

confirmed.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated file uploads (nopriv AJAX)
confirmed.data_flow: Same && logic bug as the image upload handler: `if ( !wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-' . $timestamp ) && is_user_logged_in() )` at line 691 means nonce is never checked for unauthenticated callers of wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload. The caller can omit or send a garbage nonce and still reach UM()->uploader()->upload_file(). Impact is bounded by wp_handle_upload MIME allowlist (no PHP), and by the subsequent mode-check at line 715 (blocks profile mode for guests), confining unauthenticated uploads to register-mode forms and the temp directory. Rate limiting at line 661 provides partial mitigation.

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The logic bug at line 691 of class-files.php is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` evaluates to false for any unauthenticated caller because `is_user_logged_in()` is false, short-circuiting the AND. This means the nonce check is completely skipped for guests, even though the intent of the surrounding `if ( $um_file_upload_nonce )` block is to enforce it.

The AJAX action `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload` is confirmed registered in class-ajax-common.php line 52, making this reachable by unauthenticated callers.

Downstream checks that bound (but do not eliminate) impact: (1) `mode=profile` is blocked for guests at line 715 — only `mode=register` is reachable. (2) If `user_id` is non-null AND mode is `register`, an error is returned (line 720-723). (3) `wp_handle_upload` enforces MIME type checking against `$allowed_file_mimes` built from the form field's `allowed_types` config — PHP execution is not possible in a default install. (4) Files land in the `temp/` subdirectory (class-uploader.php lines 279-280). (5) Rate limiting applies (line 661).

No effective check saves the nonce bypass itself. An unauthenticated attacker can upload files matching the field's allowed MIME types to the temp directory without any nonce, simply by knowing a valid published `um_form` post ID, the field key, and submitting `set_mode=register`. The form post ID is not secret — it is a public WordPress post. The bypass is exploitable on any default install that has a published Ultimate Member registration form with a file upload field configured. The severity (medium) and auth_required (none) in the claim are both correct.

confirmed.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated image uploads (ajax_image_upload)
confirmed.vuln_class: Broken Authentication / Missing CSRF Defense for Unauthenticated Users
confirmed.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] are read at line 517-518. The nonce is checked at line 519 with: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, "um_upload_nonce-{$timestamp}" ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. Because both operands must be true for the block to execute, and `is_user_logged_in()` is false for unauthenticated callers, the entire condition is always false — the nonce is never verified. Execution reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_image( $_FILES[$id], null, $id ) at line 638 with no form-session proof. The uploaded file lands in wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ which is web-accessible. File type is validated by wp_handle_upload + validate_upload against allowed MIME types (PNG/JPEG/JPG/WEBP by default), so PHP shells cannot be stored, but the server receives and stores arbitrary image-typed files without any authenticated session token.

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The logic bug at line 519 is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` uses AND instead of the clearly intended OR, so for any unauthenticated caller `is_user_logged_in()` is false and the entire condition short-circuits to false — the nonce check is dead code for the nopriv path.

The downstream guards do not save it for the register-mode path. An unauthenticated caller who supplies user_id=null and mode=register passes every subsequent check: line 531 only blocks when user_id is non-null and the caller is not logged in; line 542 only blocks profile mode; line 547 only blocks register mode when user_id is non-null. The remaining checks (lines 552-610) require a valid published um_form post with matching mode and a valid field key — all conditions any real registration form on the target site trivially satisfies.

Execution reaches `UM()->uploader()->upload_image()` at line 638 with no session proof whatsoever.

The one effective defense is MIME type enforcement via `wp_handle_upload` + allowed-types filter (PNG/JPEG/WEBP by default), which prevents PHP shell storage and thus rules out RCE. Files land in the web-accessible wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ directory. A soft rate-limiter exists (line 459) but is per-IP and bypassable with IP rotation.

Net result: unauthenticated actors can upload arbitrary image-typed files to a predictable web-accessible temp path with no form-session token required. This enables disk exhaustion DoS, phishing/malware asset hosting, and bypass of any upload quota tied to authenticated sessions. Severity is medium (no code execution path, but real unauthenticated upload capability). The claimed auth level of none is correct — the hook is registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload (class-ajax-common.php line 55).

confirmed.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated file uploads (ajax_file_upload)
confirmed.vuln_class: Broken Authentication / Missing CSRF Defense for Unauthenticated Users
confirmed.data_flow: Identical logic error to line 519: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-' . $timestamp ) && is_user_logged_in() )` at line 691 silently skips nonce enforcement for unauthenticated callers. With `user_id` omitted and `set_mode=register`, the code reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_file( $_FILES[$id], null, $id ) at line 794. Allowed file types are configured per-field (pdf/doc/zip/etc). The only remaining gate is the optional `ajax_nopriv_rate_limit` setting (10 req/min per IP by default, disabled by default). An unauthenticated attacker can store arbitrary files of allowed MIME types in the server temp directory at will.

verdict.auth_required: none — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is registered at class-ajax-common.php line 52, confirmed unauthenticated
verdict.reasoning:
The nonce logic at line 691 is `(!wp_verify_nonce(...)) && is_user_logged_in()`. For unauthenticated callers is_user_logged_in() returns false, making the entire condition false regardless of nonce validity — the error branch is never reached. This is a confirmed logical bypass.

All downstream gates were traced: the user_id checks (lines 698-707) are skipped when user_id is null (omitted); the mode checks (lines 715-723) block profile mode for guests but allow register mode with null user_id; form validation (lines 725-747) requires a valid published um_form post with matching mode — a trivially satisfied precondition on any UM install with a registration form; field key validation (lines 774-788) requires a legitimate file-type field in that form's custom_fields — also trivially satisfied by reading the rendered registration form HTML.

Execution reaches upload_file(FILES[$id], null, $id) at line 794. Inside class-uploader.php upload_file(), wp_handle_upload() is called with a MIME allowlist built from the form field's allowed_types setting (pdf, doc, zip, etc.). WordPress core's wp_handle_upload enforces MIME type via wp_check_filetype_and_ext() which validates both extension and actual file content — PHP and executable files are blocked. Files land in the standard uploads directory under a unique filename.

The rate limiting gate at line 661 calls is_rate_limited('upload_file'), but class-functions.php line 63 shows the feature is disabled by default (ajax_nopriv_rate_limit option is off), so there is no throttle on default installs.

The vulnerability is real: unauthenticated users can upload arbitrary files of the field-configured allowed types (pdf/doc/zip/docx etc.) with no authentication or CSRF token. The saving grace is that WP core MIME enforcement prevents PHP/executable upload, so RCE is not achievable. The impact is unauthenticated file storage abuse (disk exhaustion, malicious document hosting, potential phishing payloads). Severity is correctly medium — not critical because executables are blocked. Auth level (none) is correct.

confirmed.title: Unauthenticated usermeta enumeration via ajax_select_options members_directory branch
confirmed.data_flow: An unauthenticated caller POSTs: action=um_select_options, nonce=<any frontend-page nonce>, child_callback=<whitelisted function>, members_directory=yes, child_name=<any usermeta key>. After the callback allowlist check (lines 146-161, which gates child_callback only), line 180-188 executes: SELECT DISTINCT meta_value FROM wp_usermeta WHERE meta_key = %s using $arr_options['post']['child_name'] directly (no whitelist on child_name). The returned $values_array is then used at lines 194-199 to intersect with the whitelisted callback's own output. The actual DB values are not directly echoed, but the intersection acts as a Boolean oracle: items present in both the callback return and the DB appear in the response, items absent from the DB are filtered out. An attacker can enumerate whether arbitrary private meta values exist for any user by iterating child_name over sensitive keys (e.g. _um_status, _um_member_since, wp_capabilities, etc.).

verdict.auth_required: none — um-frontend-nonce is embedded in localized JS on any page loading UM's frontend scripts, making it freely obtainable by any visitor with a single GET request before the POST
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real but the claim significantly overstates what is exposed. Key findings:

1. REGISTRATION CONFIRMED: wp_ajax_nopriv_um_select_options is registered at class-ajax-common.php line 36. check_ajax_nonce() verifies 'um-frontend-nonce', which is seeded into localized JS (frontend/class-enqueue.php line 125) on any UM-enabled page — freely obtainable by any unauthenticated visitor.

2. NO SQL INJECTION: The claim's data flow is correct but the SQL risk is nil. $arr_options['post']['child_name'] flows into $wpdb->prepare() with a %s placeholder (class-form.php lines 180-187). wpdb::prepare() properly parameterizes the query; child_name is treated as a string literal, not concatenated. This is not a SQL injection.

3. ORACLE IS REAL BUT HIGHLY CONSTRAINED: The intersection at lines 194-199 does create a Boolean oracle, but only for values that the whitelisted callback already publicly returns. The attacker cannot enumerate arbitrary meta_values — they can only confirm whether values already known from the callback's public output (e.g., country names, role labels) happen to exist as the stored value for a given meta_key in the DB. For truly sensitive keys (wp_capabilities stores serialized PHP arrays, tokens, hashes), the values never overlap with human-readable dropdown labels, so no sensitive data leaks in practice.

4. CALLBACK ALLOWLIST IS A REAL CONTROL: child_callback is strictly gated by an admin-configured allowlist (lines 146-161) AND a blacklist check (line 163). child_name has no such whitelist, but since child_name is only used as a meta_key in a parameterized query and the results are intersected against the (already public) callback output, the damage surface is the intersection oracle described above.

5. AUTH LEVEL CORRECTION: The claim states 'none' which is correct in effect — the nonce is CSRF protection only, not authentication. An attacker visits any UM page, extracts the nonce from the JS, then POSTs directly. No account needed.

BOTTOM LINE: Real issue — unauthenticated callers can probe whether specific (publicly-known) option values exist in the DB for any usermeta key, by iterating child_name. Severity 'low' is accurate. This is not the SQL injection or direct data dump the claim implies; it is a weak oracle constrained by the whitelisted callback's fixed output set. The rate-limiting (is_rate_limited, class-functions.php line 58) adds a partial but optional mitigation for unauthenticated callers.

confirmed.title: Broken nonce gate allows unauthenticated file upload bypass (ajax_image_upload)
confirmed.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Callers
confirmed.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_imageupload with any or no nonce → line 515-516: $um_image_upload_nonce = apply_filters('um_image_upload_nonce', true) → line 519: if (!wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in()) — condition is false when caller is not logged in, so nonce failure is silently ignored → downstream user_id check at line 531 blocks editing existing users but is bypassed by omitting user_id (register mode) → line 638: UM()->uploader()->upload_image($_FILES[$id], $user_id=null, $id) — file is written to the temp directory

verdict.auth_required: None — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload; unauthenticated access confirmed
verdict.reasoning: The nonce bypass at line 519 is genuine: `if ( !wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in() )` uses `&&`, so when the caller is unauthenticated `is_user_logged_in()` returns false and the whole condition is false — invalid or absent nonces are silently ignored for unauthenticated callers. The hook is confirmed registered as `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload` in class-ajax-common.php line 55, so the handler is reachable without authentication. With `user_id` omitted (null) and `set_mode=register`, all user-based authorization checks at lines 526, 531, and 542 are bypassed. The check at line 547 (`null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode`) also does not fire because `null !== null` is false. However, the vuln is NOT exploitable on a default install: lines 552-610 enforce that the caller must supply a valid published `um_form` post ID whose `custom_fields` contain the supplied `$id` key as an image field, and the default registration form ships only with text/password fields (user_login, user_email, user_password, first_name, last_name — no image fields). On real-world installs that add an image/file upload field to a registration form (a common customization), the path holds and an unauthenticated attacker can upload files. The upload sink (`wp_handle_upload` at line 355 of class-uploader.php) enforces MIME type checking against an allowed list defaulting to JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF, so PHP shell upload is blocked and the file lands in the WordPress temp upload directory. The impact is unauthenticated arbitrary image upload to server disk, not remote code execution. The claimed severity of high is overstated — medium is correct because: no code execution path exists through the image-only MIME filter, no user account is affected (null user_id), and exploitation requires a non-default form configuration. The auth bypass mechanism itself is confirmed real.

confirmed.title: Broken nonce gate allows unauthenticated file upload bypass (ajax_file_upload)
confirmed.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Callers
confirmed.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_fileupload → line 686-687: $um_file_upload_nonce = apply_filters('um_file_upload_nonce', true) → line 691: if (!wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-'.$timestamp) && is_user_logged_in()) — the && means nonce failure exits only when the caller IS logged in; an unauthenticated caller always passes this gate regardless of nonce validity → line 704 blocks existing-user edits but omitting user_id skips that branch → line 794: UM()->uploader()->upload_file($_FILES[$id], $user_id=null, $id) — arbitrary file is written to temp directory

verdict.auth_required: None — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is reachable by unauthenticated callers. Requires a published UM registration form with a file-upload field (common default config).
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The full exploit path holds:

1. HOOK: wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is confirmed registered at class-ajax-common.php:52, binding ajax_file_upload() to unauthenticated POST requests.

2. NONCE BYPASS (line 691): `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in() )` — the && operator means the error branch fires only when BOTH the nonce fails AND the caller is logged in. For unauthenticated callers, is_user_logged_in() is always false, collapsing the entire condition to false, so the wp_send_json_error() die path is never reached. The nonce is completely ineffective for the nopriv case.

3. USER_ID GATE (line 704): `if ( $user_id && ! is_user_logged_in() )` — only blocks if user_id is non-null. Attacker sends user_id empty/absent → $user_id = null → check skipped.

4. MODE GATE (line 715): `if ( ! is_user_logged_in() && 'profile' === $mode )` — blocked for profile mode only. Attacker sends set_mode=register → skipped.

5. REGISTER+USER_ID GATE (line 720): `if ( null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode )` — only fires when user_id is non-null. With null user_id + register mode, skipped.

6. FORM VALIDATION (lines 725-783): Requires a valid published um_form post of mode=register containing the specified field key in custom_fields. This is a prerequisite but is satisfied by any default UM installation that includes a file/avatar upload field on the registration form (standard/common configuration).

7. FIELD EDIT CHECK (line 785): profile-mode only, skipped.

8. FILE UPLOAD SINK (line 794): wp_handle_upload() is called with the attacker-supplied $_FILES entry. The uploader enforces a MIME whitelist derived from the field's allowed_types setting (lines 541-552), validated by WordPress core's wp_check_filetype_and_ext(). This blocks PHP extension uploads — arbitrary webshell/RCE is NOT achievable through this path. WordPress core's own MIME enforcement is the saving check for the RCE subclass.

9. REMAINING IMPACT: An unauthenticated attacker can upload files of any type permitted by the registration form's file field (images, PDFs, Office documents, etc.) without any authentication or valid nonce. This constitutes unauthenticated arbitrary file upload within allowed MIME types. Severity is correctly high: storage abuse, potential for storing malicious payloads that social-engineer users or trigger client-side attacks, and disk exhaustion. The claimed 'high' severity is appropriate even absent RCE.

The auth_required field in the claim is correct. The severity of 'high' is justified. The specific bug — the && logic inversion on the nonce check at line 691 that renders the nonce meaningless for nopriv callers — is a real, exploitable logic flaw.

confirmed.title: Missing authentication check in ajax_remove_file allows unauthenticated deletion of temp uploads
confirmed.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_remove_file with mode=register (or empty user_id) → line 267: UM()->check_ajax_nonce() enforces a frontend nonce, but the nonce 'um-frontend-nonce' is embedded in every public page → line 289: if ($mode == 'register' || empty($_POST['user_id'])) — no capability check is performed in this branch → line 291: $is_temp = um_is_temp_upload($src) verifies the URL resolves inside the temp directory → line 309: $this->delete_file($src) → unlink($is_temp). An unauthenticated attacker who obtains a victim's pending-registration temp-file URL (e.g., via network sniffing or information disclosure) and a valid frontend nonce (present on any public page) can permanently delete that file, disrupting in-progress registrations.

verdict.auth_required: None — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_remove_file is registered; a valid um-frontend-nonce (obtainable by any visitor loading a UM-enabled page) satisfies the only check
verdict.reasoning: The attack path holds end-to-end. Hook registration at class-ajax-common.php lines 49-50 confirms both wp_ajax and wp_ajax_nopriv variants are wired to ajax_remove_file. The sole gate is check_ajax_nonce() which verifies a wp_verify_nonce against 'um-frontend-nonce' — this nonce is localized into JS on any public UM page (class-enqueue.php line 125), so any unauthenticated visitor can obtain a valid one for their session. In the register branch (line 289: mode==register or empty user_id) there is no capability check and no ownership verification. delete_file() calls um_is_temp_upload() which constrains the unlink() strictly to paths inside the /ultimatemember/temp/ directory — this eliminates arbitrary file deletion but does not prevent deletion of any file in that directory whose URL the attacker knows. The practical exploit requires the attacker to know a victim's temp file URL; temp filenames are typically randomized so brute-forcing is impractical, but the URL could be leaked via referrer headers, logs, or shared-session scenarios. The vulnerability is real (missing authorization, no ownership check), but the impact is limited to disruption of in-progress registrations — no user data, no arbitrary files, no privilege escalation. Severity is low rather than medium: the constrained scope (temp dir only), the prerequisite of knowing the target URL, and the limited impact (availability of a pending registration upload, not user account data) all reduce it below medium. The nonce is not the saving check — it is trivially obtainable. The um_is_temp_upload path-confinement is the only real defense, and it only limits scope, not access.

confirmed.title: CSRF: Logout page executes logout with no nonce verification
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in user (victim must be authenticated; attacker is unauthenticated)
confirmed.data_flow: Attacker crafts a page with or a zero-click GET request. Browser automatically attaches victim's cookies. template_redirect fires -> logout_page() at line 67 checks um_is_core_page('logout') — true because the URL matches the UM logout page ID — then at line 69 is_user_logged_in() returns true. No nonce is checked anywhere. wp_destroy_current_session() (line 74) and wp_logout() (line 75) execute, terminating the victim's session. The logout link generated in includes/core/um-actions-user.php:18 appends only redirect_to, never a _wpnonce parameter.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in user (victim must be authenticated; attacker needs no credentials)
verdict.reasoning:
Verified end-to-end by reading the actual source.

CONFIRMED VULNERABLE PATH:
- class-logout.php:23 registers logout_page() on template_redirect at priority 10000.
- logout_page() checks only um_is_core_page('logout') (pure post-ID match, no token) and is_user_logged_in(). No nonce, no referer, no capability check beyond authentication.
- wp_destroy_current_session() + wp_logout() execute on a plain, unauthenticated GET request to the UM logout page URL (lines 74-75, 79-80, 103-104).
- um-actions-user.php:18 generates logout links with only a redirect_to param — no _wpnonce ever appended.
- Exhaustive grep across the entire includes/ tree confirms zero nonce verification anywhere near the logout path.

WHY WP CORE DEFENSES DO NOT SAVE IT:
- WP core's own logout (wp-login.php?action=logout) is protected by a logoutconfirm nonce, but UM bypasses that route entirely, calling wp_logout() directly from template_redirect.
- SameSite=Lax (default since WP 5.8) blocks cross-origin SUBRESOURCE requests (img, script, fetch no-cors) but explicitly allows cookies on top-level GET navigations — which is precisely what this attack requires. A hidden form auto-submitting via GET, window.location redirect, or even a meta-refresh delivers the request with full cookies under Lax.
- Sites on WordPress < 5.8, or with custom cookie configurations, have no SameSite protection at all.

SEVERITY CONFIRMATION: Medium is correct. Logout CSRF forces session termination (annoyance, interruption of work, possible exposure of subsequent unauthenticated state) but does not achieve data exfiltration or privilege escalation on its own.

confirmed.title: ajax_paginate: missing capability check — any authenticated user can invoke it
confirmed.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (no nopriv registration, but no current_user_can() check either; nonce only proves session, not authorization)
confirmed.data_flow: wp_ajax_um_ajax_paginate → ajax_paginate() → UM()->check_ajax_nonce() verifies nonce only, no current_user_can() check → do_action("um_ajax_load_posts__{$hook}", $args) fires with caller-supplied args

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (wp_ajax_ prefix enforces authentication; no nopriv variant registered; nonce is session-tied via wp_verify_nonce against 'um-frontend-nonce')
verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is real but significantly overstated. Confirmed facts: (1) The action is registered only as wp_ajax_um_ajax_paginate (not nopriv), so WordPress core requires a valid logged-in session — unauthenticated callers get -1. (2) check_ajax_nonce() calls wp_verify_nonce() against 'um-frontend-nonce', which is a genuine cryptographic check tied to the user session; it does not prove authorization but does prove an authenticated session received a nonce from a rendered page. (3) There is no current_user_can() check — any subscriber-level user who has a valid frontend nonce can invoke this endpoint. (4) The $hook input is sanitized via sanitize_key() (reduces to [a-z0-9_-]), preventing hook-name injection. (5) Critical: grep across the entire plugin source finds ZERO registered handlers for the um_ajax_load_posts__ hook family — it is a documented extension point only. In a default install the do_action() fires with no listeners, the output buffer captures nothing, and die('') returns empty. There is no exploitable sink in the plugin itself. The missing capability check is a real design flaw (any authenticated user can trigger this endpoint, not just privileged users), but the actual impact in a default install is an empty response. The vulnerability only becomes exploitable if a third-party plugin registers a um_ajax_load_posts__{hook} handler that trusts the unsanitized $args array. Severity is low (not medium) for a default install; the 'high' confidence claim is wrong because there is no exploitable default sink. The auth_required level stated in the claim is correct.

confirmed.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated users on image upload (wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload)
confirmed.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Requests
confirmed.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] → wp_verify_nonce() result ANDed with is_user_logged_in() → for unauthenticated callers is_user_logged_in() is always false so the entire condition (line 519: `! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in()`) is always false → nonce enforcement is skipped → $_FILES[$id] → UM()->uploader()->upload_image() → wp_handle_upload() → temp directory under wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The nonce logic at line 519 of class-files.php reads: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, "um_upload_nonce-{$timestamp}" ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. For an unauthenticated caller, `is_user_logged_in()` is always false, making the entire AND condition false regardless of nonce validity. The nonce-failure branch is never entered, so unauthenticated requests entirely bypass nonce enforcement — this is not a speculative reading; it is the only possible runtime outcome.

The downstream auth checks do NOT close the gap for all paths: (1) line 531 `if ( $user_id && ! is_user_logged_in() )` only fires when `$user_id` is truthy; an attacker omits or empties `user_id` so `$user_id = null`, and the check is skipped. (2) Line 542 blocks `mode=profile` for unauthenticated users. (3) Line 547 blocks `mode=register` only when `$user_id !== null` — with `$user_id = null` (empty POST field), this condition is false and register mode is allowed through. Therefore an unauthenticated attacker can send `user_id=` (empty), `mode=register`, a valid `set_id` pointing to a published `um_form` post, and a file upload, and reach `UM()->uploader()->upload_image()` at line 638.

The `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload` hook is confirmed registered in class-ajax-common.php line 55, with a comment explicitly noting it is "Enabled image uploading on registration form" — confirming this nopriv path is intentional but misconfigured.

What prevents complete RCE: `upload_image()` calls `wp_handle_upload()` with a `mimes` override restricting accepted types to JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF (with server-side MIME inspection by WP core). Arbitrary PHP or executable files cannot be uploaded. The destination is a temp directory under `wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/` with a unique filename callback, so files land in a known but randomly-named location. There is no direct code execution path.

Impact is real but constrained: an unauthenticated attacker can pollute the temp upload directory with arbitrary image-type files (SSRF-by-image is excluded by WP's MIME check), consuming disk space and potentially probing for image-processing vulnerabilities. The severity of medium and auth_required of none are both correct as stated in the claim.

confirmed.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated users on file upload (wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload)
