all_with_verdicts.title: Missing capability check on wp_ajax_um_get_users allows any logged-in user to enumerate all usernames and IDs
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (including Subscriber role)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: 1. Attacker logs in as a low-privilege user (e.g. Subscriber) and visits any wp-admin page (e.g. /wp-admin/). 2. admin_enqueue_scripts fires unconditionally (class-enqueue.php:566 -> load_global_scripts() at line 494), embedding a valid 'um-admin-nonce' into the JS global um_admin_scripts.nonce for every admin page visitor. 3. Attacker POST-requests admin-ajax.php with action=um_get_users&nonce=<obtained_nonce>&page=N. 4. get_users() (class-users.php:19) calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-admin-functions.php:21-27), which only verifies the nonce action 'um-admin-nonce' — no current_user_can() check is ever performed. 5. WP_User_Query runs with fields=['ID','user_login'] (class-users.php:26-40) and returns paginated results covering every user account on the site. Adding avatar=1 to the request appends avatar URLs. Total user count is also returned, enabling full enumeration.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (Subscriber role or above)
verdict.reasoning:
The full exploit chain holds without any effective defense:

1. AJAX hook registration (class-users.php:16): `add_action('wp_ajax_um_get_users', ...)` — `wp_ajax_` prefix requires login but imposes no capability check. No `nopriv` variant exists, so unauthenticated users are blocked, but that is the only gate.

2. Nonce acquisition: `load_global_scripts()` (class-enqueue.php:494-526) calls `wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce')` and stores it in `um_admin_scripts.nonce` unconditionally. This method is called without any capability guard at line 566 from `admin_enqueue_scripts`. WordPress core redirects Subscribers (who hold the `read` capability) to `/wp-admin/profile.php` — a genuine admin page — where `admin_enqueue_scripts` fires and the nonce is injected into the page. A Subscriber simply needs to load `/wp-admin/` once to obtain a valid nonce.

3. Nonce-only "auth" (class-admin-functions.php:21-27): `check_ajax_nonce()` calls `wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce')` and nothing else. There is no `current_user_can()` call anywhere in the call chain — not in `check_ajax_nonce()`, not in `get_users()`, not in any filter on `um_get_users_list_ajax_args`.

4. Data exposure (class-users.php:26-54): `WP_User_Query` runs with `fields => ['ID', 'user_login']`, paginated, covering the entire user table. `get_total()` reveals the full user count. Adding `avatar=1` to the request appends avatar URLs. No `exclude`, `role__in`, or ownership scoping is applied by default.

No effective mitigating control exists: the nonce only proves the requester visited an admin page (which Subscribers can do), not that they hold any administrative capability. The stated severity of medium is appropriate — this is user enumeration (IDs, logins, avatars), not direct privilege escalation or data modification. The auth_required level of "any logged-in user including Subscriber" is precisely correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated image uploads (nopriv AJAX)
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] are read, but the rejection branch at line 519 is `if ( !wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` — the && means the check only fires when the user IS logged in; for the wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload path is_user_logged_in() is always false so the nonce is never verified. Control then reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_image() which writes to the publicly-accessible temp directory (wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/).

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The && logic flaw at line 519 is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` never fires for unauthenticated requests because is_user_logged_in() is always false on the nopriv path, making the nonce check completely inert for those requests.

The full exploit path to upload_image() at line 638 is clear for an unauthenticated attacker using user_id=empty and set_mode=register: (1) $user_id=null bypasses the line-531 login gate since it tests `$user_id &&`; (2) mode=register bypasses the line-542 profile-mode block; (3) null $user_id bypasses the line-547 register+user_id block; (4) the additional per-field permission checks at lines 612-631 are wrapped in `if ('profile' === $mode)` and are entirely skipped for register mode; (5) a valid published um_form post ID is required but present by default on any Ultimate Member install; (6) a valid field key from that form is required but is rendered in page HTML.

The one partial mitigation that prevents the worst outcome: wp_handle_upload() is called with MIME type validation enforced (JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF whitelist by default), which prevents PHP webshell upload. This limits impact to unauthenticated arbitrary image file write to wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ — a publicly-accessible directory with no .htaccess protection found in the codebase.

The rate limiting (is_rate_limited at line 459) is opt-in and disabled by default — it explicitly returns false when the ajax_nopriv_rate_limit option is not set (line 63 of class-functions.php).

Severity of medium is appropriate given that arbitrary code execution is prevented by the MIME check, but unauthenticated image writes to a public directory enable disk exhaustion, potential EXIF-based information disclosure, and server-side image processing abuse. Auth level of none is correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated file uploads (nopriv AJAX)
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Same && logic bug as the image upload handler: `if ( !wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-' . $timestamp ) && is_user_logged_in() )` at line 691 means nonce is never checked for unauthenticated callers of wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload. The caller can omit or send a garbage nonce and still reach UM()->uploader()->upload_file(). Impact is bounded by wp_handle_upload MIME allowlist (no PHP), and by the subsequent mode-check at line 715 (blocks profile mode for guests), confining unauthenticated uploads to register-mode forms and the temp directory. Rate limiting at line 661 provides partial mitigation.

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The logic bug at line 691 of class-files.php is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` evaluates to false for any unauthenticated caller because `is_user_logged_in()` is false, short-circuiting the AND. This means the nonce check is completely skipped for guests, even though the intent of the surrounding `if ( $um_file_upload_nonce )` block is to enforce it.

The AJAX action `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload` is confirmed registered in class-ajax-common.php line 52, making this reachable by unauthenticated callers.

Downstream checks that bound (but do not eliminate) impact: (1) `mode=profile` is blocked for guests at line 715 — only `mode=register` is reachable. (2) If `user_id` is non-null AND mode is `register`, an error is returned (line 720-723). (3) `wp_handle_upload` enforces MIME type checking against `$allowed_file_mimes` built from the form field's `allowed_types` config — PHP execution is not possible in a default install. (4) Files land in the `temp/` subdirectory (class-uploader.php lines 279-280). (5) Rate limiting applies (line 661).

No effective check saves the nonce bypass itself. An unauthenticated attacker can upload files matching the field's allowed MIME types to the temp directory without any nonce, simply by knowing a valid published `um_form` post ID, the field key, and submitting `set_mode=register`. The form post ID is not secret — it is a public WordPress post. The bypass is exploitable on any default install that has a published Ultimate Member registration form with a file upload field configured. The severity (medium) and auth_required (none) in the claim are both correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated image uploads (ajax_image_upload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Broken Authentication / Missing CSRF Defense for Unauthenticated Users
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload (class-ajax-common.php line 55)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] are read at line 517-518. The nonce is checked at line 519 with: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, "um_upload_nonce-{$timestamp}" ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. Because both operands must be true for the block to execute, and `is_user_logged_in()` is false for unauthenticated callers, the entire condition is always false — the nonce is never verified. Execution reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_image( $_FILES[$id], null, $id ) at line 638 with no form-session proof. The uploaded file lands in wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ which is web-accessible. File type is validated by wp_handle_upload + validate_upload against allowed MIME types (PNG/JPEG/JPG/WEBP by default), so PHP shells cannot be stored, but the server receives and stores arbitrary image-typed files without any authenticated session token.

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The logic bug at line 519 is confirmed: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in() )` uses AND instead of the clearly intended OR, so for any unauthenticated caller `is_user_logged_in()` is false and the entire condition short-circuits to false — the nonce check is dead code for the nopriv path.

The downstream guards do not save it for the register-mode path. An unauthenticated caller who supplies user_id=null and mode=register passes every subsequent check: line 531 only blocks when user_id is non-null and the caller is not logged in; line 542 only blocks profile mode; line 547 only blocks register mode when user_id is non-null. The remaining checks (lines 552-610) require a valid published um_form post with matching mode and a valid field key — all conditions any real registration form on the target site trivially satisfies.

Execution reaches `UM()->uploader()->upload_image()` at line 638 with no session proof whatsoever.

The one effective defense is MIME type enforcement via `wp_handle_upload` + allowed-types filter (PNG/JPEG/WEBP by default), which prevents PHP shell storage and thus rules out RCE. Files land in the web-accessible wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ directory. A soft rate-limiter exists (line 459) but is per-IP and bypassable with IP rotation.

Net result: unauthenticated actors can upload arbitrary image-typed files to a predictable web-accessible temp path with no form-session token required. This enables disk exhaustion DoS, phishing/malware asset hosting, and bypass of any upload quota tied to authenticated sessions. Severity is medium (no code execution path, but real unauthenticated upload capability). The claimed auth level of none is correct — the hook is registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload (class-ajax-common.php line 55).

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated file uploads (ajax_file_upload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Broken Authentication / Missing CSRF Defense for Unauthenticated Users
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload (class-ajax-common.php line 52)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Identical logic error to line 519: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-' . $timestamp ) && is_user_logged_in() )` at line 691 silently skips nonce enforcement for unauthenticated callers. With `user_id` omitted and `set_mode=register`, the code reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_file( $_FILES[$id], null, $id ) at line 794. Allowed file types are configured per-field (pdf/doc/zip/etc). The only remaining gate is the optional `ajax_nopriv_rate_limit` setting (10 req/min per IP by default, disabled by default). An unauthenticated attacker can store arbitrary files of allowed MIME types in the server temp directory at will.

verdict.auth_required: none — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is registered at class-ajax-common.php line 52, confirmed unauthenticated
verdict.reasoning:
The nonce logic at line 691 is `(!wp_verify_nonce(...)) && is_user_logged_in()`. For unauthenticated callers is_user_logged_in() returns false, making the entire condition false regardless of nonce validity — the error branch is never reached. This is a confirmed logical bypass.

All downstream gates were traced: the user_id checks (lines 698-707) are skipped when user_id is null (omitted); the mode checks (lines 715-723) block profile mode for guests but allow register mode with null user_id; form validation (lines 725-747) requires a valid published um_form post with matching mode — a trivially satisfied precondition on any UM install with a registration form; field key validation (lines 774-788) requires a legitimate file-type field in that form's custom_fields — also trivially satisfied by reading the rendered registration form HTML.

Execution reaches upload_file(FILES[$id], null, $id) at line 794. Inside class-uploader.php upload_file(), wp_handle_upload() is called with a MIME allowlist built from the form field's allowed_types setting (pdf, doc, zip, etc.). WordPress core's wp_handle_upload enforces MIME type via wp_check_filetype_and_ext() which validates both extension and actual file content — PHP and executable files are blocked. Files land in the standard uploads directory under a unique filename.

The rate limiting gate at line 661 calls is_rate_limited('upload_file'), but class-functions.php line 63 shows the feature is disabled by default (ajax_nopriv_rate_limit option is off), so there is no throttle on default installs.

The vulnerability is real: unauthenticated users can upload arbitrary files of the field-configured allowed types (pdf/doc/zip/docx etc.) with no authentication or CSRF token. The saving grace is that WP core MIME enforcement prevents PHP/executable upload, so RCE is not achievable. The impact is unauthenticated file storage abuse (disk exhaustion, malicious document hosting, potential phishing payloads). Severity is correctly medium — not critical because executables are blocked. Auth level (none) is correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Unauthenticated usermeta enumeration via ajax_select_options members_directory branch
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_select_options (class-ajax-common.php line 36)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: An unauthenticated caller POSTs: action=um_select_options, nonce=<any frontend-page nonce>, child_callback=<whitelisted function>, members_directory=yes, child_name=<any usermeta key>. After the callback allowlist check (lines 146-161, which gates child_callback only), line 180-188 executes: SELECT DISTINCT meta_value FROM wp_usermeta WHERE meta_key = %s using $arr_options['post']['child_name'] directly (no whitelist on child_name). The returned $values_array is then used at lines 194-199 to intersect with the whitelisted callback's own output. The actual DB values are not directly echoed, but the intersection acts as a Boolean oracle: items present in both the callback return and the DB appear in the response, items absent from the DB are filtered out. An attacker can enumerate whether arbitrary private meta values exist for any user by iterating child_name over sensitive keys (e.g. _um_status, _um_member_since, wp_capabilities, etc.).

verdict.auth_required: none — um-frontend-nonce is embedded in localized JS on any page loading UM's frontend scripts, making it freely obtainable by any visitor with a single GET request before the POST
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real but the claim significantly overstates what is exposed. Key findings:

1. REGISTRATION CONFIRMED: wp_ajax_nopriv_um_select_options is registered at class-ajax-common.php line 36. check_ajax_nonce() verifies 'um-frontend-nonce', which is seeded into localized JS (frontend/class-enqueue.php line 125) on any UM-enabled page — freely obtainable by any unauthenticated visitor.

2. NO SQL INJECTION: The claim's data flow is correct but the SQL risk is nil. $arr_options['post']['child_name'] flows into $wpdb->prepare() with a %s placeholder (class-form.php lines 180-187). wpdb::prepare() properly parameterizes the query; child_name is treated as a string literal, not concatenated. This is not a SQL injection.

3. ORACLE IS REAL BUT HIGHLY CONSTRAINED: The intersection at lines 194-199 does create a Boolean oracle, but only for values that the whitelisted callback already publicly returns. The attacker cannot enumerate arbitrary meta_values — they can only confirm whether values already known from the callback's public output (e.g., country names, role labels) happen to exist as the stored value for a given meta_key in the DB. For truly sensitive keys (wp_capabilities stores serialized PHP arrays, tokens, hashes), the values never overlap with human-readable dropdown labels, so no sensitive data leaks in practice.

4. CALLBACK ALLOWLIST IS A REAL CONTROL: child_callback is strictly gated by an admin-configured allowlist (lines 146-161) AND a blacklist check (line 163). child_name has no such whitelist, but since child_name is only used as a meta_key in a parameterized query and the results are intersected against the (already public) callback output, the damage surface is the intersection oracle described above.

5. AUTH LEVEL CORRECTION: The claim states 'none' which is correct in effect — the nonce is CSRF protection only, not authentication. An attacker visits any UM page, extracts the nonce from the JS, then POSTs directly. No account needed.

BOTTOM LINE: Real issue — unauthenticated callers can probe whether specific (publicly-known) option values exist in the DB for any usermeta key, by iterating child_name. Severity 'low' is accurate. This is not the SQL injection or direct data dump the claim implies; it is a weak oracle constrained by the whitelisted callback's fixed output set. The rate-limiting (is_rate_limited, class-functions.php line 58) adds a partial but optional mitigation for unauthenticated callers.

all_with_verdicts.title: Broken nonce gate allows unauthenticated file upload bypass (ajax_image_upload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Callers
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: None — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_imageupload with any or no nonce → line 515-516: $um_image_upload_nonce = apply_filters('um_image_upload_nonce', true) → line 519: if (!wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in()) — condition is false when caller is not logged in, so nonce failure is silently ignored → downstream user_id check at line 531 blocks editing existing users but is bypassed by omitting user_id (register mode) → line 638: UM()->uploader()->upload_image($_FILES[$id], $user_id=null, $id) — file is written to the temp directory

verdict.auth_required: None — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload; unauthenticated access confirmed
verdict.reasoning: The nonce bypass at line 519 is genuine: `if ( !wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in() )` uses `&&`, so when the caller is unauthenticated `is_user_logged_in()` returns false and the whole condition is false — invalid or absent nonces are silently ignored for unauthenticated callers. The hook is confirmed registered as `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload` in class-ajax-common.php line 55, so the handler is reachable without authentication. With `user_id` omitted (null) and `set_mode=register`, all user-based authorization checks at lines 526, 531, and 542 are bypassed. The check at line 547 (`null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode`) also does not fire because `null !== null` is false. However, the vuln is NOT exploitable on a default install: lines 552-610 enforce that the caller must supply a valid published `um_form` post ID whose `custom_fields` contain the supplied `$id` key as an image field, and the default registration form ships only with text/password fields (user_login, user_email, user_password, first_name, last_name — no image fields). On real-world installs that add an image/file upload field to a registration form (a common customization), the path holds and an unauthenticated attacker can upload files. The upload sink (`wp_handle_upload` at line 355 of class-uploader.php) enforces MIME type checking against an allowed list defaulting to JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF, so PHP shell upload is blocked and the file lands in the WordPress temp upload directory. The impact is unauthenticated arbitrary image upload to server disk, not remote code execution. The claimed severity of high is overstated — medium is correct because: no code execution path exists through the image-only MIME filter, no user account is affected (null user_id), and exploitation requires a non-default form configuration. The auth bypass mechanism itself is confirmed real.

all_with_verdicts.title: Broken nonce gate allows unauthenticated file upload bypass (ajax_file_upload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Callers
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: None — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_fileupload → line 686-687: $um_file_upload_nonce = apply_filters('um_file_upload_nonce', true) → line 691: if (!wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-'.$timestamp) && is_user_logged_in()) — the && means nonce failure exits only when the caller IS logged in; an unauthenticated caller always passes this gate regardless of nonce validity → line 704 blocks existing-user edits but omitting user_id skips that branch → line 794: UM()->uploader()->upload_file($_FILES[$id], $user_id=null, $id) — arbitrary file is written to temp directory

verdict.auth_required: None — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is reachable by unauthenticated callers. Requires a published UM registration form with a file-upload field (common default config).
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The full exploit path holds:

1. HOOK: wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload is confirmed registered at class-ajax-common.php:52, binding ajax_file_upload() to unauthenticated POST requests.

2. NONCE BYPASS (line 691): `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in() )` — the && operator means the error branch fires only when BOTH the nonce fails AND the caller is logged in. For unauthenticated callers, is_user_logged_in() is always false, collapsing the entire condition to false, so the wp_send_json_error() die path is never reached. The nonce is completely ineffective for the nopriv case.

3. USER_ID GATE (line 704): `if ( $user_id && ! is_user_logged_in() )` — only blocks if user_id is non-null. Attacker sends user_id empty/absent → $user_id = null → check skipped.

4. MODE GATE (line 715): `if ( ! is_user_logged_in() && 'profile' === $mode )` — blocked for profile mode only. Attacker sends set_mode=register → skipped.

5. REGISTER+USER_ID GATE (line 720): `if ( null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode )` — only fires when user_id is non-null. With null user_id + register mode, skipped.

6. FORM VALIDATION (lines 725-783): Requires a valid published um_form post of mode=register containing the specified field key in custom_fields. This is a prerequisite but is satisfied by any default UM installation that includes a file/avatar upload field on the registration form (standard/common configuration).

7. FIELD EDIT CHECK (line 785): profile-mode only, skipped.

8. FILE UPLOAD SINK (line 794): wp_handle_upload() is called with the attacker-supplied $_FILES entry. The uploader enforces a MIME whitelist derived from the field's allowed_types setting (lines 541-552), validated by WordPress core's wp_check_filetype_and_ext(). This blocks PHP extension uploads — arbitrary webshell/RCE is NOT achievable through this path. WordPress core's own MIME enforcement is the saving check for the RCE subclass.

9. REMAINING IMPACT: An unauthenticated attacker can upload files of any type permitted by the registration form's file field (images, PDFs, Office documents, etc.) without any authentication or valid nonce. This constitutes unauthenticated arbitrary file upload within allowed MIME types. Severity is correctly high: storage abuse, potential for storing malicious payloads that social-engineer users or trigger client-side attacks, and disk exhaustion. The claimed 'high' severity is appropriate even absent RCE.

The auth_required field in the claim is correct. The severity of 'high' is justified. The specific bug — the && logic inversion on the nonce check at line 691 that renders the nonce meaningless for nopriv callers — is a real, exploitable logic flaw.

all_with_verdicts.title: HTTP Response Header Injection via unsanitized filename and MIME type in download handlers
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: None — download_routing is a template_redirect action, publicly accessible with a valid nonce for any user ID
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: At upload time, $_FILES[$id]['name'] (original filename) and MIME type are stored verbatim into user meta key '{field_key}_metadata' → at download time download_routing() (line 115) reads get_user_meta($user_id, $field_key.'_metadata', true) → $originalname = $file_info['original_name'] (line 197/242) and $type = $file_info['type'] (line 198/243) — neither is sanitized — both are interpolated raw into header() calls: header('Content-Type: '.$type) and header('Content-Disposition: inline; filename="'.$originalname.'"'). A user who uploads a file with a newline in the original filename or content-type injects arbitrary HTTP response headers, enabling cache poisoning or reflected response-splitting attacks. Affects both image_download (line 200-207) and file_download (line 245-252).

verdict.auth_required: A valid nonce is required for download_routing(); the nonce embeds user_id + form_id and is generated only when a download link is rendered to a visitor with sufficient view permissions. This is not "None" — an unauthenticated attacker cannot forge a nonce without first obtaining one from a rendered page that exposes the link.
verdict.reasoning:
The claim's core mechanism — CRLF injection through `original_name` and `type` in `header()` calls — is neutralized by PHP's own runtime.

CONFIRMED CODE FACTS:

1. `original_name` source: `$uploadedfile['name']` (i.e., `$_FILES[$id]['name']`) is stored verbatim in `{field_key}_metadata` with no sanitization (class-uploader.php lines 406 and 573). This is genuinely attacker-controlled.

2. `type` source: Derived from `wp_check_filetype()` applied to the server-side basename after `wp_handle_upload()` renames/validates the file (lines 403 and 568). `wp_check_filetype()` returns values from WordPress's internal `$wp_mime_types` whitelist. The value is NOT taken from `$_FILES[...]['type']`. Attacker cannot control this field.

3. Both values are interpolated raw into `header()` at class-files.php lines 201–202 and 246–247. No sanitization wrapper is applied before `header()`.

4. DECISIVE DEFENSE — PHP runtime: Live test confirms that PHP's `header()` function silently drops any header containing `\r` or `\n` characters and the header is never sent. `headers_list()` returns an empty array after an attempted CRLF injection. This protection has been in PHP since version 5.1.2 (2006), well before any WordPress version that could run this plugin. On PHP 8.x (required by modern WP), the behavior is confirmed: the header is silently discarded.

Therefore, even though `original_name` is stored without sanitization and reaches `header()` unsanitized, the PHP interpreter itself blocks the injection at the point of the `header()` call. No attacker can split response headers regardless of what they place in the filename. The claim's attack path is neutralized by a platform-level defense the reviewer missed.

The `type` field is also not attacker-controlled (it comes from WP's MIME whitelist, not from the upload request), which eliminates that half of the claimed attack surface entirely.

The vulnerability as described cannot be exploited on any PHP version that WordPress supports (7.4+).

all_with_verdicts.title: Missing authentication check in ajax_remove_file allows unauthenticated deletion of temp uploads
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: None — registered on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_remove_file
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to wp-admin/admin-ajax.php?action=um_remove_file with mode=register (or empty user_id) → line 267: UM()->check_ajax_nonce() enforces a frontend nonce, but the nonce 'um-frontend-nonce' is embedded in every public page → line 289: if ($mode == 'register' || empty($_POST['user_id'])) — no capability check is performed in this branch → line 291: $is_temp = um_is_temp_upload($src) verifies the URL resolves inside the temp directory → line 309: $this->delete_file($src) → unlink($is_temp). An unauthenticated attacker who obtains a victim's pending-registration temp-file URL (e.g., via network sniffing or information disclosure) and a valid frontend nonce (present on any public page) can permanently delete that file, disrupting in-progress registrations.

verdict.auth_required: None — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_remove_file is registered; a valid um-frontend-nonce (obtainable by any visitor loading a UM-enabled page) satisfies the only check
verdict.reasoning: The attack path holds end-to-end. Hook registration at class-ajax-common.php lines 49-50 confirms both wp_ajax and wp_ajax_nopriv variants are wired to ajax_remove_file. The sole gate is check_ajax_nonce() which verifies a wp_verify_nonce against 'um-frontend-nonce' — this nonce is localized into JS on any public UM page (class-enqueue.php line 125), so any unauthenticated visitor can obtain a valid one for their session. In the register branch (line 289: mode==register or empty user_id) there is no capability check and no ownership verification. delete_file() calls um_is_temp_upload() which constrains the unlink() strictly to paths inside the /ultimatemember/temp/ directory — this eliminates arbitrary file deletion but does not prevent deletion of any file in that directory whose URL the attacker knows. The practical exploit requires the attacker to know a victim's temp file URL; temp filenames are typically randomized so brute-forcing is impractical, but the URL could be leaked via referrer headers, logs, or shared-session scenarios. The vulnerability is real (missing authorization, no ownership check), but the impact is limited to disruption of in-progress registrations — no user data, no arbitrary files, no privilege escalation. Severity is low rather than medium: the constrained scope (temp dir only), the prerequisite of knowing the target URL, and the limited impact (availability of a pending registration upload, not user account data) all reduce it below medium. The nonce is not the saving check — it is trivially obtainable. The um_is_temp_upload path-confinement is the only real defense, and it only limits scope, not access.

all_with_verdicts.title: CSRF: Logout page executes logout with no nonce verification
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in user (victim must be authenticated; attacker is unauthenticated)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Attacker crafts a page with or a zero-click GET request. Browser automatically attaches victim's cookies. template_redirect fires -> logout_page() at line 67 checks um_is_core_page('logout') — true because the URL matches the UM logout page ID — then at line 69 is_user_logged_in() returns true. No nonce is checked anywhere. wp_destroy_current_session() (line 74) and wp_logout() (line 75) execute, terminating the victim's session. The logout link generated in includes/core/um-actions-user.php:18 appends only redirect_to, never a _wpnonce parameter.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in user (victim must be authenticated; attacker needs no credentials)
verdict.reasoning:
Verified end-to-end by reading the actual source.

CONFIRMED VULNERABLE PATH:
- class-logout.php:23 registers logout_page() on template_redirect at priority 10000.
- logout_page() checks only um_is_core_page('logout') (pure post-ID match, no token) and is_user_logged_in(). No nonce, no referer, no capability check beyond authentication.
- wp_destroy_current_session() + wp_logout() execute on a plain, unauthenticated GET request to the UM logout page URL (lines 74-75, 79-80, 103-104).
- um-actions-user.php:18 generates logout links with only a redirect_to param — no _wpnonce ever appended.
- Exhaustive grep across the entire includes/ tree confirms zero nonce verification anywhere near the logout path.

WHY WP CORE DEFENSES DO NOT SAVE IT:
- WP core's own logout (wp-login.php?action=logout) is protected by a logoutconfirm nonce, but UM bypasses that route entirely, calling wp_logout() directly from template_redirect.
- SameSite=Lax (default since WP 5.8) blocks cross-origin SUBRESOURCE requests (img, script, fetch no-cors) but explicitly allows cookies on top-level GET navigations — which is precisely what this attack requires. A hidden form auto-submitting via GET, window.location redirect, or even a meta-refresh delivers the request with full cookies under Lax.
- Sites on WordPress < 5.8, or with custom cookie configurations, have no SameSite protection at all.

SEVERITY CONFIRMATION: Medium is correct. Logout CSRF forces session termination (annoyance, interruption of work, possible exposure of subsequent unauthenticated state) but does not achieve data exfiltration or privilege escalation on its own.

all_with_verdicts.title: Missing capability check in admin AJAX handler allows any logged-in user to enumerate arbitrary usermeta values
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Broken Access Control / Unauthorized Information Disclosure
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (subscriber or any UM role)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: POST $_REQUEST['key'] (sanitize_text_field, line 3234) -> show_filter() (line 3237) -> case 'select': $wpdb->prepare('SELECT DISTINCT meta_value FROM wp_usermeta WHERE meta_key = %s', $attrs['metakey']) (lines 861-869) -> wp_send_json_success() returning all distinct meta values (line 3239). The handler is registered only as wp_ajax_ (authenticated), verifies um-admin-nonce via UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce() (line 3231), but has no current_user_can() check. Any logged-in user can produce a valid um-admin-nonce (WordPress nonces are user-session-tied; any user can call wp_create_nonce for any action string). The um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role guard (um-actions-wpadmin.php:12) skips enforcement when DOING_AJAX is defined, so the admin-ajax.php endpoint is reachable by subscribers.

verdict.auth_required: wp-admin access (effectively Administrator or Editor with admin page access)
verdict.reasoning:
The data-flow and missing current_user_can() check are real, but the exploit chain breaks at the nonce step.

WHAT IS TRUE:
- `wp_ajax_um_member_directory_default_filter_settings` is registered authenticated-only (no nopriv variant) at class-admin-ajax-hooks.php:30.
- `default_filter_settings()` calls only `UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce()` (class-admin-functions.php:21-27), which is `wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-admin-nonce')`. There is no `current_user_can()` call.
- `show_filter()` for case 'select' does run `$wpdb->prepare('SELECT DISTINCT meta_value FROM wp_usermeta WHERE meta_key = %s', $attrs['metakey'])` (lines 861-869), a real usermeta enumeration query.
- `um_block_wpadmin_by_user_role` (um-actions-wpadmin.php:12) does skip its redirect when `DOING_AJAX` is defined, so admin-ajax.php is reachable by subscribers in principle.

WHERE THE CLAIM FAILS - the nonce:
The claim asserts 'any logged-in user can produce a valid um-admin-nonce (WordPress nonces are user-session-tied; any user can call wp_create_nonce for any action string).' While it is technically correct that wp_create_nonce() does not check capabilities, a subscriber needs a SERVER-SIDE execution context to call it. The `um-admin-nonce` is created in exactly two places: (1) class-enqueue.php:501 via `admin_enqueue_scripts` hook (wp-admin only), and (2) several admin modal templates under includes/admin/templates/. None of these are accessible to subscribers - they are all wp-admin pages. There is no frontend page, no REST endpoint, no wp_ajax_nopriv_ handler, and no other vehicle in the plugin that calls wp_create_nonce('um-admin-nonce') in a subscriber-accessible context. A subscriber cannot construct a cryptographically valid nonce without triggering server-side PHP execution on a page that creates it.

Therefore, a subscriber cannot pass the wp_verify_nonce() check and cannot reach the SQL query. The nonce functions as an effective de facto admin-page gate even though it was intended only as CSRF protection.

RESIDUAL CONCERN (code quality, not an independent vuln): The missing current_user_can('manage_options') call is a defense-in-depth gap. If any separate vulnerability (e.g., a plugin that exposes the admin nonce to lower-privilege users, or a multisite misconfiguration granting admin access) allows a subscriber to obtain the nonce, this endpoint would then leak all distinct meta_values for any meta_key. This is worth noting as a hardening recommendation, but it is NOT independently exploitable on a default install.

all_with_verdicts.title: ajax_paginate: missing capability check — any authenticated user can invoke it
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (no nopriv registration, but no current_user_can() check either; nonce only proves session, not authorization)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: wp_ajax_um_ajax_paginate → ajax_paginate() → UM()->check_ajax_nonce() verifies nonce only, no current_user_can() check → do_action("um_ajax_load_posts__{$hook}", $args) fires with caller-supplied args

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (wp_ajax_ prefix enforces authentication; no nopriv variant registered; nonce is session-tied via wp_verify_nonce against 'um-frontend-nonce')
verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is real but significantly overstated. Confirmed facts: (1) The action is registered only as wp_ajax_um_ajax_paginate (not nopriv), so WordPress core requires a valid logged-in session — unauthenticated callers get -1. (2) check_ajax_nonce() calls wp_verify_nonce() against 'um-frontend-nonce', which is a genuine cryptographic check tied to the user session; it does not prove authorization but does prove an authenticated session received a nonce from a rendered page. (3) There is no current_user_can() check — any subscriber-level user who has a valid frontend nonce can invoke this endpoint. (4) The $hook input is sanitized via sanitize_key() (reduces to [a-z0-9_-]), preventing hook-name injection. (5) Critical: grep across the entire plugin source finds ZERO registered handlers for the um_ajax_load_posts__ hook family — it is a documented extension point only. In a default install the do_action() fires with no listeners, the output buffer captures nothing, and die('') returns empty. There is no exploitable sink in the plugin itself. The missing capability check is a real design flaw (any authenticated user can trigger this endpoint, not just privileged users), but the actual impact in a default install is an empty response. The vulnerability only becomes exploitable if a third-party plugin registers a um_ajax_load_posts__{hook} handler that trusts the unsanitized $args array. Severity is low (not medium) for a default install; the 'high' confidence claim is wrong because there is no exploitable default sink. The auth_required level stated in the claim is correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Unsanitized $_REQUEST['args'] passed to dynamic action hook and output echoed unescaped
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Reflected XSS / Unsanitized Input to Dynamic Hook
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in WordPress user (same access control as above)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_REQUEST['args'] (line 44, no sanitization) → passed as $args to do_action("um_ajax_load_posts__{$hook}", $args) (line 63) → ob_get_clean() captures any output → die($output) at line 67 with no escaping (developer TODO comment acknowledges missing wp_kses). If any hooked callback reflects $args contents in HTML, this is a stored or reflected XSS sink.

verdict.auth_required: Not exploitable — requires valid authenticated session AND valid wp_verify_nonce('um-frontend-nonce') token, which is session-tied and defeats CSRF-driven XSS
verdict.reasoning:
REFUTED on three independent grounds:

1. AUTH GATE — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_ajax_paginate is NOT registered (class-ajax-common.php line 41 has only wp_ajax_um_ajax_paginate). WordPress silently returns 0 to unauthenticated callers and never invokes ajax_paginate(). This contradicts the claim that any logged-in user can exploit it — it requires a valid WP session, but more importantly the XSS path still requires defeating the nonce.

2. NONCE GATE DEFEATS CSRF-XSS — UM()->check_ajax_nonce() (class-functions.php line 41-48) calls wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um-frontend-nonce') and dies with wp_send_json_error() on failure. The nonce is generated per-session via wp_create_nonce() and embedded in the frontend JS bundle. An attacker cannot forge this nonce for a victim's session, so the classic CSRF-to-XSS path is blocked. Only the authenticated user themselves can send a valid nonce — making any resulting XSS self-XSS, not a security vulnerability.

3. NO DEFAULT SINK — The actual output depends entirely on what callbacks are hooked to do_action("um_ajax_load_posts__{$hook}", $args). The $hook is attacker-controlled but is sanitized with sanitize_key() (line 42). No default UM core handler is registered to um_ajax_load_posts__* that reflects $args into HTML output. The @todo wp_kses comment is a code quality note, not evidence of a real reflection path in the default install.

The data-flow observation (unsanitized $args reaches die($output)) is technically correct as a code pattern, but the reviewer failed to account for wp_verify_nonce() as the blocking control. The specific check/sanitizer that saves it: wp_verify_nonce() called unconditionally at line 36 before any request data is processed.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated users on image upload (wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Requests
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] → wp_verify_nonce() result ANDed with is_user_logged_in() → for unauthenticated callers is_user_logged_in() is always false so the entire condition (line 519: `! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in()`) is always false → nonce enforcement is skipped → $_FILES[$id] → UM()->uploader()->upload_image() → wp_handle_upload() → temp directory under wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The nonce logic at line 519 of class-files.php reads: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, "um_upload_nonce-{$timestamp}" ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. For an unauthenticated caller, `is_user_logged_in()` is always false, making the entire AND condition false regardless of nonce validity. The nonce-failure branch is never entered, so unauthenticated requests entirely bypass nonce enforcement — this is not a speculative reading; it is the only possible runtime outcome.

The downstream auth checks do NOT close the gap for all paths: (1) line 531 `if ( $user_id && ! is_user_logged_in() )` only fires when `$user_id` is truthy; an attacker omits or empties `user_id` so `$user_id = null`, and the check is skipped. (2) Line 542 blocks `mode=profile` for unauthenticated users. (3) Line 547 blocks `mode=register` only when `$user_id !== null` — with `$user_id = null` (empty POST field), this condition is false and register mode is allowed through. Therefore an unauthenticated attacker can send `user_id=` (empty), `mode=register`, a valid `set_id` pointing to a published `um_form` post, and a file upload, and reach `UM()->uploader()->upload_image()` at line 638.

The `wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload` hook is confirmed registered in class-ajax-common.php line 55, with a comment explicitly noting it is "Enabled image uploading on registration form" — confirming this nopriv path is intentional but misconfigured.

What prevents complete RCE: `upload_image()` calls `wp_handle_upload()` with a `mimes` override restricting accepted types to JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF (with server-side MIME inspection by WP core). Arbitrary PHP or executable files cannot be uploaded. The destination is a temp directory under `wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/` with a unique filename callback, so files land in a known but randomly-named location. There is no direct code execution path.

Impact is real but constrained: an unauthenticated attacker can pollute the temp upload directory with arbitrary image-type files (SSRF-by-image is excluded by WP's MIME check), consuming disk space and potentially probing for image-processing vulnerabilities. The severity of medium and auth_required of none are both correct as stated in the claim.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce check bypassed for unauthenticated users on file upload (wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Authentication Bypass / Missing Nonce Enforcement for Unauthenticated Requests
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_POST['_wpnonce'] and $_POST['timestamp'] → wp_verify_nonce() result ANDed with is_user_logged_in() → condition at line 691 (`! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in()`) is always false for unauthenticated callers → nonce never checked → $_FILES[$id] → UM()->uploader()->upload_file() → wp_handle_upload() with field-defined MIME allowlist → temp directory under wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The nonce bypass is confirmed real. Line 691 of class-files.php reads: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-' . $timestamp ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. For an unauthenticated caller, `is_user_logged_in()` is always false, so the compound AND condition is always false and the error branch never executes. The nonce is never enforced for nopriv requests.

The full attacker path holds for a `register`-mode upload with no user_id:
- Line 699: `$user_id && is_user_logged_in()` — passes (user_id null, logged-in false).
- Line 704: `$user_id && ! is_user_logged_in()` — passes (user_id null).
- Line 715: `! is_user_logged_in() && 'profile' === $mode` — passes (mode is 'register').
- Line 720: `null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode` — passes (user_id is null).
- Lines 725–747: Form must be a published `um_form` post with mode='register'. Registration forms are published on the site's registration page and their post ID and field keys are visible in the page HTML.
- Line 794: `UM()->uploader()->upload_file( $_FILES[$id], $user_id, $id )` is reached and calls `wp_handle_upload()` with `test_form => false`.

What limits severity (not a full refutation, but keeps it medium not critical):
1. MIME allowlist: `wp_handle_upload` is called with `mimes` constrained to the field definition's `allowed_types` (typically PDF, DOC, etc. for file fields). WP core's `wp_check_filetype_and_ext` performs real MIME sniffing, so renaming a PHP file to `.pdf` will be caught.
2. Upload destination is `wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/` — a web-accessible directory, but WordPress itself places an `.htaccess` in `wp-content/uploads/` blocking PHP execution on Apache. This is a platform-level control, not present on nginx installs without similar config.
3. Rate limiting: defaults to 10 requests/minute/IP (class-config.php line 743 shows `ajax_nopriv_rate_limit => true`), but this is IP-based and trivially bypassed.
4. No `.htaccess` is created by this plugin for the temp dir specifically — only the parent uploads dir protection from WP core applies.

The vulnerability is real: an unauthenticated attacker can upload arbitrary files of field-permitted MIME types to a web-accessible temp directory with no nonce check. The stated auth_required=none and severity=medium are both correct. The finding is not a critical RCE because PHP execution is blocked by WP core MIME enforcement and typical Apache upload directory protection, but it is a genuine unauthenticated arbitrary file write to a web-accessible location on hosts without PHP blocking in uploads (e.g., nginx without additional hardening), and the nonce bypass is unambiguous.

all_with_verdicts.title: Unauthenticated file upload bypass: nonce guard short-circuits for non-logged-in callers on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload (class-ajax-common.php line 55-56)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_FILES[$id] (user-supplied multipart upload) → ajax_image_upload() [class-files.php:458] → nonce check at line 519 is `(!wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in())`: when caller is unauthenticated is_user_logged_in() returns false, boolean AND makes full expression false, guard never triggers → UM()->uploader()->upload_image($_FILES[$id], ...) [class-uploader.php:307] → wp_handle_upload() writes file to wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ and returns URL in JSON response

verdict.auth_required: none — unauthenticated via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload, but only when user_id is null and mode is "register"
verdict.reasoning:
The nonce bypass at class-files.php line 519 is real: `(!wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in())` means when the caller is unauthenticated, is_user_logged_in() is false, the AND short-circuits to false, and the guard never fires. The claim's logic is correct.

However, subsequent checks narrow the exploitable path significantly:
- Line 531: blocks unauthenticated callers who supply a non-null user_id ("Please login to edit this user").
- Line 542: blocks unauthenticated callers in "profile" mode.

The exploitable path requires: POST user_id=empty (null), POST set_mode="register", and a valid published um_form post ID (set_id) that contains an image field matching the supplied key. Registration forms are commonly present on UM installs and their IDs are publicly discoverable.

Critically, wp_handle_upload is called with mimes restricted to image types (JPG/JPEG/PNG/GIF by default, enforced via validate_upload -> validate_image_data and the mimes override array at class-uploader.php lines 337-349). WordPress also performs its own MIME sniffing check. This prevents PHP/shell file upload and eliminates the direct RCE path.

The real impact is: unauthenticated users can upload arbitrary image files to wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ without rate limiting beyond UM's own is_rate_limited() check. This enables disk exhaustion DoS, potential SSRF via image parsing libraries, and storage abuse, but not direct code execution.

Severity should be medium rather than high: the nonce bypass is confirmed and the access control failure is real, but the image-only MIME restriction is an effective sanitizer against the highest-severity outcomes. The claim's "high" severity is overstated; the correct severity is medium given no RCE path exists on a default install.

all_with_verdicts.title: Unauthenticated file upload bypass: nonce guard short-circuits for non-logged-in callers on wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload (class-ajax-common.php line 52-53)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_FILES[$id] (user-supplied multipart upload) → ajax_file_upload() [class-files.php:660] → nonce check at line 691 is `(!wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in())`: when caller is unauthenticated is_user_logged_in() returns false, full condition is always false, guard never triggers → requires set_mode=register + valid published um_form post ID + field key in form's custom_fields (all discoverable by attacker) → UM()->uploader()->upload_file($_FILES[$id], ...) [class-uploader.php:524] → wp_handle_upload() writes file to ultimatemember/temp/ — allowed types include zip/rar/doc/pdf/mp3; URL returned in JSON; exploitable for DoS via storage exhaustion and unauthenticated arbitrary-type file write (WP core MIME sentry blocks .php execution)

verdict.auth_required: none — wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload; unauthenticated callers reach the handler
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real but severity should be downgraded from high to medium because RCE is blocked.

CONFIRMED PATH:
1. wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload (class-ajax-common.php:52) routes unauthenticated POST requests to ajax_file_upload().
2. Nonce guard at line 691 is `(!wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in())`. For an unauthenticated caller is_user_logged_in() returns false, making the entire condition false — the guard never fires. This is a genuine logic error: the intended guard is an OR (block if nonce fails OR if not logged in), but it was coded as AND.
3. user_id must be null (or absent) to pass the line 704 check — consistent with register mode.
4. Mode must be 'register', not 'profile' (line 715 blocks nopriv+profile). Register mode passes.
5. A valid published um_form post of post_type='um_form' with mode='register' must exist and the $_POST['key'] field must exist in that form's custom_fields. These are real prerequisites but discoverable: um_form posts are published on public pages during normal plugin use. An attacker can enumerate them via the public registration page's form_id and field keys embedded in page HTML.
6. After all gates, UM()->uploader()->upload_file() is called (class-uploader.php:524) which calls wp_handle_upload() with a 'mimes' allowlist derived from the field's allowed_types (pdf, doc, docx, zip, rar, mp3, jpg, png, etc.).
7. WP core's MIME sentry in wp_handle_upload blocks .php and other executable types even if sent — no RCE path exists through this vector.
8. File lands in wp-content/uploads/ultimatemember/temp/ — world-readable URL is returned in JSON response.
9. Rate limiting (is_rate_limited, line 661) applies 10 requests/minute/IP by default (ajax_nopriv_rate_limit=true per class-config.php:743). This is a soft mitigation for DoS but does not prevent the upload itself.

WHAT SAVES IT FROM HIGH: WP core enforces MIME type checking inside wp_handle_upload via check_filetype_and_ext() which cross-validates the file's binary signature against the extension and the allowed mimes array. PHP/executable files cannot land on disk. The impact is therefore limited to: (a) storage exhaustion DoS at 10 files/min/IP (rate-limited but bypassable with IP rotation), and (b) unauthenticated write of allowed types (zip/doc/pdf/mp3) to temp/ dir with a public URL. No RCE, no privilege escalation.

SEVERITY CORRECTION: The claim states 'high'. The correct severity is medium. The nonce bypass is real and exploitable without authentication, but the absence of any code execution path caps the impact. Storage DoS with rate limiting as the only guard, plus unauthenticated arbitrary file write of non-executable types, is medium severity by standard CVSS-like reasoning (low confidentiality/integrity impact, possible availability impact).

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated image uploads
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Missing Authentication / CSRF bypass on file upload
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_imageupload → ajax_image_upload() → line 519: nonce check is `! wp_verify_nonce($nonce, ...) && is_user_logged_in()` which evaluates to `! (valid) && false = false`, so the nonce block never triggers → upload proceeds to UM()->uploader()->upload_image() with $user_id=null (register mode). File type is validated by wp_check_filetype but no CSRF token is required.

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real. The action wp_ajax_nopriv_um_imageupload is confirmed registered at /includes/core/class-ajax-common.php line 55, meaning unauthenticated POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_imageupload reaches ajax_image_upload() with zero WP-level auth gate.

The nonce check at line 519 is: `if ( ! wp_verify_nonce( $nonce, ... ) && is_user_logged_in() )`. For an unauthenticated attacker, is_user_logged_in() is false, so the whole condition is false regardless of the nonce result — the error block never fires. No CSRF token is ever enforced for unauthenticated callers.

The downstream checks do not save it for the register-mode path:
- Line 526 (`$user_id && is_user_logged_in()`) — skipped when $user_id is null (unauthenticated, no user_id in POST).
- Line 531 (`$user_id && ! is_user_logged_in()`) — skipped when $user_id is null.
- Line 542 (`! is_user_logged_in() && 'profile' === $mode`) — blocks profile mode only, not register.
- Line 547 (`null !== $user_id && 'register' === $mode`) — skipped when $user_id is null.

The only remaining gate is that a valid published um_form post of type 'register' must exist (lines 552–574). This is the default state of any site using Ultimate Member's registration form — it is not a meaningful defense.

After passing those checks, the upload reaches UM()->uploader()->upload_image() at line 638 with $user_id=null (register mode). The file type is validated by wp_check_filetype within the uploader, but no CSRF token is required. An unauthenticated attacker can upload arbitrary images (JPEG, PNG, GIF) to the server's UM upload directory without any session or token requirement.

The specific bug: the nonce guard uses && (both conditions must be true to block) rather than || (either bad nonce OR unauthenticated should block). This is a classic logic error that silently disables CSRF protection for the entire unauthenticated code path. Severity is medium rather than critical because file-type validation in the uploader restricts uploads to image MIME types, limiting direct impact to storage abuse / potential SSRF-adjacent issues rather than RCE.

all_with_verdicts.title: Nonce verification skipped for unauthenticated file uploads
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Missing Authentication / CSRF bypass on file upload
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated POST to admin-ajax.php?action=um_fileupload → ajax_file_upload() → line 691: nonce check is `! wp_verify_nonce($nonce, 'um_upload_nonce-'.$timestamp) && is_user_logged_in()` which short-circuits to false when caller is unauthenticated → upload proceeds to UM()->uploader()->upload_file() with $user_id=null (register mode). Allowed file types (pdf, doc, zip, mp3, jpg, etc.) are enforced by wp_check_filetype but no CSRF protection exists for the upload itself.

verdict.auth_required: none — registered via wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload; unauthenticated callers bypass nonce check entirely due to && is_user_logged_in() short-circuit
verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability is real. The boolean logic at line 691 of class-files.php is definitively broken: `! wp_verify_nonce(...) && is_user_logged_in()` evaluates to false for unauthenticated callers regardless of nonce validity, so no error is thrown and execution continues. The wp_ajax_nopriv_um_fileupload hook (class-ajax-common.php:52) confirms the handler is reachable without authentication. The full exploit path passes all subsequent guards: passing user_id empty (null) bypasses the ownership and user-logged-in checks at lines 699-707; passing set_mode=register bypasses the profile-mode auth check at line 715; the form_id is trivially discoverable since it is rendered in HTML data-set_id attributes on the public registration page (class-fields.php:3266). No WP core defense intervenes — wp_check_filetype and wp_handle_upload do restrict uploads to the field's configured allowed_types (pdf, doc, zip, jpg, etc.) and will block PHP execution, so RCE is not achievable. The actual impact is unauthenticated upload of allowed-type files to the server's uploads directory (disk exhaustion, unwanted content storage). This is a genuine missing authentication / CSRF bypass on file upload with medium severity: no RCE, but real unauthorized write access to the filesystem. The claimed severity of medium and auth_required of none are both correct.

all_with_verdicts.title: Missing nonce verification on default action path — any logged-in user can fire um_action_user_request_hook with arbitrary action and user_id
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Missing CSRF / Access Control on Extensibility Hook
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in user (Subscriber+)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_REQUEST['um_action'] (line 55, sanitize_key only) → switch default (line 181) → do_action('um_action_user_request_hook', $action, $user_id) (line 201). The only pre-switch guard is empty() on $_REQUEST['nonce'] (line 34), which only checks presence. wp_verify_nonce() is never called for the default case. $user_id comes from $_REQUEST['uid'] via absint() (line 40). The hook receives the attacker-controlled $action string and $user_id integer with no server-side nonce or capability validation having been applied.
all_with_verdicts.entry_point: wp_loaded action → actions_listener() → switch default branch. Triggered by any GET/POST request with um_action=<custom_value>&nonce=<any_nonempty_string>&uid=<target_uid>. No AJAX registration required; fires on every frontend page load.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in user (Subscriber+)
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is confirmed real. Code analysis of /home/openclaw/Repos/Research/.claude/worktrees/woo-security-review/plugins/src/ultimate-member/includes/frontend/class-actions-listener.php:

CONFIRMED FACTS:
1. Line 30: `is_user_logged_in()` — any logged-in user can proceed (Subscriber+ confirmed).
2. Line 34: `empty($_REQUEST['nonce'])` — only checks presence, not cryptographic validity. Any non-empty string (e.g., nonce=x) passes this check.
3. Lines 58–180: Every named case (approve_user, reject_user, delete, switch_user, etc.) calls `wp_verify_nonce()` with a specific action string before acting.
4. Lines 181–202 (default branch): No `wp_verify_nonce()` call whatsoever. The hook fires immediately: `do_action('um_action_user_request_hook', $action, $user_id)`.
5. The docblock at line 186 explicitly delegates nonce responsibility to third-party implementors: "Note: Please verify nonce and redirect after action individually in 3rd-party handler."

GUARDS THAT DO NOT SAVE IT:
- Line 43 (user_exists check): Only validates the uid refers to a real user — not an authorization check.
- Line 47 (`get_current_user_id() === $user_id` returns): Only blocks self-targeting; any other user is reachable.
- Line 51 (super_admin check): Only blocks targeting super admins; all regular users remain reachable.
- `sanitize_key()` on $action (line 55): Sanitizes format only, does not restrict which action strings are accepted.
- `absint()` on $user_id (line 40): Type-coerces only, no authorization.

ATTACK PATH: GET /?um_action=custom_val&nonce=x&uid=<target_uid> from any logged-in session reaches line 201 with no valid nonce verification applied. The hook receives attacker-controlled $action (sanitize_key'd) and $user_id (absint'd).

ON A DEFAULT INSTALL: With no third-party hooks registered to um_action_user_request_hook, do_action() is a no-op. However, Ultimate Member is an extensibility framework and this hook is the documented extension point for third-party user action handlers. Any plugin/code hooking um_action_user_request_hook and trusting that framework-level nonce verification has already occurred is immediately vulnerable — the docblock creates a false sense of security by saying "verify nonce individually" while the framework itself models the opposite behavior for all built-in cases. This is a real design-level vulnerability in the extensibility surface.

CSRF NUANCE: Because is_user_logged_in() is required, a pure CSRF attack from an unauthenticated attacker is blocked. However, a logged-in attacker can directly forge the request. Additionally, CSRF against another logged-in user requires that victim to be logged in, limiting the pure CSRF vector. The primary risk is a logged-in low-privilege user (Subscriber) directly calling the endpoint with arbitrary action strings targeting other users' IDs, bypassing what third-party hook implementors may assume is pre-validated input.

Auth level is correct: Subscriber+ (any logged-in user). Severity medium is appropriate given the conditional exploitability (depends on third-party hook presence) but the framework pattern is definitively broken.

all_with_verdicts.title: Missing nonce verification in default action case allows CSRF on third-party action hooks
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in user
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_REQUEST['um_action'] (arbitrary string, sanitize_key only) + $_REQUEST['uid'] (absint, attacker-controlled victim user ID) -> switch default branch -> do_action('um_action_user_request_hook', $action, $user_id). The outer guard at line 34 only checks empty($_REQUEST['nonce']) — a non-empty but unverified nonce string satisfies it. No wp_verify_nonce() call exists in the default branch. Capability check is also absent from the default branch.

verdict.auth_required: Any logged-in user (is_user_logged_in() enforced at line 30; no elevated capability required to reach the default branch)
verdict.reasoning:
The structural claim is accurate. The outer guard at line 34 only calls empty($_REQUEST['nonce']), which is satisfied by any non-empty string — a garbage value like nonce=x passes. No wp_verify_nonce() call exists anywhere in the default branch (lines 181-202). No capability check exists in the default branch either. do_action('um_action_user_request_hook', $action, $user_id) fires unconditionally once the switch falls through to default.

The check at line 47 (get_current_user_id() === $user_id) only excludes the case where the victim IS the attacker; it does not block targeting other users. If uid is omitted or 0, $user_id is 0, and that check resolves to (current_id === 0) which is false for any logged-in user, so control still reaches the default branch.

WP core provides no additional defense here: wp_loaded fires for all authenticated page loads, no REST permission_callback applies, and the hook is a raw do_action with no built-in nonce wrapper.

What saves this from being high-severity: the base Ultimate Member plugin registers no handlers on um_action_user_request_hook — the hook is a pure third-party extension point. The docblock at lines 185-186 explicitly delegates nonce and redirect responsibility to the third-party handler. A default install with no add-ons exposes the hook but has no handler attached, so there is no concrete action performed. Exploitability is therefore conditional on a third-party handler existing that does not implement its own nonce check.

The framework design is still flawed: the architecture misleads integrators (the outer empty() check looks like an auth gate but is not), and any third-party handler that trusts the framework to have verified the nonce will be CSRF-exploitable. This is a real, verifiable vulnerability in the plugin's security architecture. Severity is medium (not high) because no handler is present in the base plugin and actual impact depends on the third-party ecosystem.

all_with_verdicts.title: Edit-mode activation for another user's profile has no CSRF protection (nonce verification explicitly suppressed)
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: Any logged-in user with UM 'can_edit_everyone' role permission
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: $_REQUEST['um_action'] = 'edit' + $_REQUEST['uid'] = <victim> -> handle_edit_screen() sets UM()->fields()->editing = true. The phpcs:disable WordPress.Security.NonceVerification comment at line 37 confirms no nonce is checked at any point in this handler. Access control (um_current_user_can('edit', ...)) is checked at line 64, so privilege escalation is blocked — but the state change (switching the page into edit mode for another user's profile) can be triggered cross-site via a crafted URL. The impact is limited to UI state, not a data write.

verdict.auth_required: N/A — not a real vulnerability
verdict.reasoning:
The claim fails on multiple grounds:

1. No persistent state change. `UM()->fields()->editing = true` is an in-memory PHP boolean that lives only for the duration of the current request. It is never written to the database, session, or any persistent store. CSRF requires a meaningful state-changing request; toggling a transient render flag is not one.

2. The access control check (line 64) fires immediately after the flag is set (line 62). Without `um_current_user_can('edit', um_profile_id())`, the code calls `um_safe_redirect()` and exits — the page never renders in edit mode for an unauthorized user, so even the claimed "UI state" impact does not materialize.

3. `$_REQUEST['uid']` does NOT set `UM()->user()->target_id`. The routing layer (class-rewrite.php `locate_user_profile`, priority 9999 on template_redirect) sets `target_id` from the URL slug/query before handle_edit_screen fires at priority 10000. The `$uid` variable in handle_edit_screen is used only for two guard checks (user_exists, is_super_admin) and then discarded. An attacker cannot redirect the edit-mode context to a victim's profile via the `uid` parameter.

4. Any actual data write (profile save) goes through a separate AJAX/form submission path in class-form.php / class-fields.php, which verifies `profile_nonce` tied to `target_id` (class-fields.php line 875: `wp_verify_nonce($post_form['profile_nonce'], 'um-profile-nonce' . UM()->user()->target_id)`). The missing nonce on handle_edit_screen does not bypass that protection.

5. The `phpcs:disable WordPress.Security.NonceVerification` comment is correct and intentional: the handler is triggered by ordinary hyperlinks (the Edit Profile button uses `add_query_arg('um_action', 'edit', $url)` per um_edit_profile_url()), which legitimately cannot carry nonces. This is standard WP practice for read/render-mode switches.

The check that saves it: the nonce on the actual form submission (`wp_verify_nonce` at class-fields.php:875) plus the ACL redirect at line 64. There is no exploitable path at any privilege level.

all_with_verdicts.title: Unauthenticated AJAX Handler Triggers Privileged Queue Execution (Nonce-Only Guard)
all_with_verdicts.vuln_class: Broken Access Control / Unauthorized Privilege Escalation
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Unauthenticated HTTP POST to admin-ajax.php?action=as_async_request_queue_runner with a valid nonce -> WP_Async_Request::maybe_handle() (line 168) -> check_ajax_referer() passes (no is_user_logged_in / current_user_can check) -> ActionScheduler_AsyncRequest_QueueRunner::handle() (ActionScheduler_AsyncRequest_QueueRunner.php line 47) -> do_action('action_scheduler_run_queue') fires all pending scheduled actions

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning: The vulnerability claim collapses on its own prerequisite: "with a valid nonce." The nonce for as_async_request_queue_runner is generated exclusively server-side in WP_Async_Request::get_query_args() and travels only in an internal wp_remote_post() loopback request the server makes to itself. It is never embedded in HTML, JavaScript, or any client-accessible endpoint. WordPress computes the nonce as hmac_sha256(nonce_tick|action|uid|session_token, AUTH_KEY+AUTH_SALT) — cryptographically unforgeable without access to wp-config.php secrets. check_ajax_referer() at line 172 of WP_Async_Request.php therefore provides effective protection despite the absence of is_user_logged_in() or current_user_can() checks, because an unauthenticated attacker has no avenue to obtain or forge the required nonce. The wp_ajax_nopriv_ hook registration means the AJAX action accepts unauthenticated requests, but every such request immediately hits check_ajax_referer() and receives wp_die(-1) without a valid server-generated nonce. This is an intentional design: the server self-dispatches queue processing by making authenticated-nonce loopback calls, not by requiring the caller to be a logged-in WordPress user. The claim misidentifies the absence of a user-role check as a missing guard, while ignoring that the nonce itself is the unforgeable credential.

all_with_verdicts.title: Stored HTML injection in admin notification email via unescaped registration field values
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none (any visitor can register)
all_with_verdicts.data_flow:
Registrant submits arbitrary HTML in a registration form field → value stored in usermeta → um_user_submitted_registration_formatted() at um-actions-register.php:130 calls um_user_submited_display($k, $title) → $v = um_user($k) returns raw usermeta string → concatenated unescaped into HTML at line 967: "

$v

" → returned as tags_replace for '{submitted_registration}' → str_replace() in um_convert_tags() at line 202 inserts raw HTML into email body → wp_mail() sends as text/html (class-mail.php:447 when email_html option is on)

verdict.auth_required: none (any visitor can register)
verdict.reasoning: The claimed data flow breaks at the sanitization step that runs before storage. In class-form.php:sanitize() (called at line 650, before post_form['submitted'] is captured at line 651), every standard field type undergoes context-correct sanitization: text/select/date/time/tel/rating/googlemap fields get sanitize_text_field(strip_shortcodes($v)) (line 935), which internally calls strip_tags() and removes all HTML markup. Multiselect/radio/checkbox fields get array_map('sanitize_text_field', ...) (line 940). Textarea fields without the html option get sanitize_textarea_field() (line 856). Textarea fields with html enabled get wp_kses() against a restricted allowlist (line 853). These sanitized values are stored in usermeta via update_user_meta($id, 'submitted', $submitted) (class-user.php:1447) and via update_profile(). By the time um_user($k) reads the value back in um_user_submited_display() and it reaches the unescaped concatenation at um-short-functions.php:967, any HTML tags have already been stripped. A registrant submitting <script>alert(1)</script> in a text field will have that stored as alert(1) — no tags survive to be injected into the email body. The missing esc_html() at line 967 is a defense-in-depth gap but not an exploitable vulnerability because the source data is already HTML-stripped at input time. The only HTML-capable path (textarea with html=true) is gated by wp_kses() which enforces a safe tag allowlist. The claim is not real.

all_with_verdicts.title: Stored XSS in admin registration-review modal via unescaped user meta values
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none for planting; manage_options to trigger
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: Registrant stores arbitrary HTML in a registration form field (usermeta) → admin opens the registration review modal in wp-admin → dynamic_modal_content() (class-admin-builder.php:1076, guarded by manage_options + nonce) calls um_fetch_user($arg1) then um_user_submitted_registration_formatted() → um_user_submited_display($k, $title) reads $v = um_user($k) from usermeta without escaping → output is 'echo $output' at class-admin-builder.php:1090 with no intervening kses or esc_html → arbitrary HTML executes in the admin browser context

verdict.auth_required: manage_options + valid admin nonce to trigger; sanitize_text_field at registration write-time defeats payload planting for all standard field types
verdict.reasoning:
The data flow breaks at two points:

1. PLANTING IS BLOCKED BY WRITE-TIME SANITIZATION. In class-form.php lines 922-935, all standard registration field types (text, select, image, file, date, time, rating, tel, etc.) are run through sanitize_text_field(strip_shortcodes($v)) before being stored as user meta. sanitize_text_field() calls wp_strip_all_tags() internally, which removes all HTML tags. A payload like <img src=x onerror=alert(1)> is stripped to plain text before it ever reaches the database. The only exception is textarea fields with the 'html' property explicitly enabled by an admin, which use wp_kses() — that still strips script/event-handler payloads.

2. TRIGGER REQUIRES manage_options + VALID NONCE. The AJAX handler wp_ajax_um_dynamic_modal_content (class-admin-ajax-hooks.php line 27) is registered only for logged-in users (no wp_ajax_nopriv_ variant). dynamic_modal_content() at class-admin-builder.php line 728 immediately calls UM()->admin()->check_ajax_nonce(), which verifies the 'um-admin-nonce' nonce (class-admin-functions.php lines 21-27). It then checks current_user_can('manage_options') at line 730. Both checks must pass. The nonce is only issued to authenticated admin sessions, so it cannot be obtained by an unauthenticated attacker.

3. THE UNESCAPED ECHO IS REAL BUT HARMLESS IN DEFAULT CONFIG. Line 967 of um-short-functions.php does echo $output with $v unescaped, which is a genuine missing esc_html() call. However, since $v = um_user($k) reads user meta that was sanitized via sanitize_text_field() at write time, no HTML survives to this point for standard fields.

The claim's stated auth_required of 'none for planting' is incorrect for default installs. The um_submitted_data_value filter (line 951) could reintroduce HTML only if a third-party plugin hooks it — that is not a default-install attack surface. The vulnerability does not hold on a default/common install.

all_with_verdicts.title: IP blocklist bypass via HTTP_CLIENT_IP spoofing
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: um_user_ip() at line 269 checks $_SERVER['HTTP_CLIENT_IP'] first (before REMOTE_ADDR) → attacker sets 'Client-IP: 1.2.3.4' HTTP header → um_submit_form_errors_hook__blockedips() at um-actions-form.php:59 compares this attacker-controlled value against the admin-configured blocked-IP list using strpos($user_ip, $ip) === 0 → blocked IP prefix never matches attacker's spoofed value → registration proceeds despite IP block

verdict.auth_required: none
verdict.reasoning: The full data flow is confirmed in the source code. um_user_ip() at includes/um-short-functions.php:269 reads $_SERVER['HTTP_CLIENT_IP'] first (the HTTP Client-IP header, fully attacker-controlled) before falling back to HTTP_X_FORWARDED_FOR and REMOTE_ADDR. No IP address validation, filter_var(FILTER_VALIDATE_IP), or sanitization is applied to the returned value before or after the apply_filters('um_user_ip', $ip) passthrough. In includes/core/um-actions-form.php:52-66, um_submit_form_errors_hook__blockedips() calls um_user_ip() and compares the result using strpos($user_ip, $ip) === 0 against each admin-configured blocked IP prefix. An attacker whose real REMOTE_ADDR is in the blocked list can set the Client-IP HTTP header to any non-blocked IP (e.g., 1.2.3.4); um_user_ip() returns that spoofed value; strpos never matches any blocked prefix; the check passes and the form submission (registration or login) proceeds. The hook fires on unauthenticated POST submissions — triggered both from um_submit_form_errors_hook() for all UM forms (login/register/profile) and directly from the authenticate filter in um-filters-login.php:47 for wp-login.php. No nonce is checked inside the blockedips callback, no current_user_can() gate exists, and WP core has no built-in mechanism to strip or validate HTTP_CLIENT_IP. The vulnerability is exploitable by any unauthenticated attacker. Impact is limited to bypassing an admin-configured IP blocklist (a non-default feature that must be actively configured), which keeps severity at low. The claimed file, line, data flow, and auth level are all accurate.

all_with_verdicts.title: CSRF on password-reset initiation — no nonce in form or handler
all_with_verdicts.auth_required: none — unauthenticated users can trigger this for any account
all_with_verdicts.data_flow: password-reset.php:42 renders <input name='_um_password_reset' value='1'> with no wp_nonce_field(). um-actions-global.php:32-48 (um_add_security_checks) adds only a honeypot. No callback to um_reset_password_page_hidden_fields emits a nonce. class-password.php:292 (form_init, hooked on template_redirect:10001) calls is_reset_request() at line 221 which checks only !empty($_POST['_um_password_reset']) with zero nonce verification anywhere in the file. On success, um_reset_password_process_hook (line 455) calls UM()->user()->password_reset($data->ID) for whatever username_b value was submitted, then redirects to checkemail.

verdict.auth_required: none — the template_redirect hook fires for unauthenticated visitors; the password-reset page is public by design
verdict.reasoning:
The vulnerability is real but the claimed severity of "medium" is overstated; "low" is more accurate.

CONFIRMED path: password-reset.php:42 renders the hidden _um_password_reset field with no wp_nonce_field(). The um_reset_password_page_hidden_fields action hook (line 64) has zero registered callbacks inside the plugin. form_init() (class-password.php:247) is hooked on template_redirect:10001 and calls is_reset_request() (line 219-222) which checks only !empty($_POST['_um_password_reset']) with an explicit phpcs:ignore WordPress.Security.NonceVerification comment. No nonce is verified anywhere in the reset-initiation path: not in form_init(), not in um_reset_password_errors_hook() (which only checks the honeypot and rate-limits), not in um_reset_password_process_hook() (line 455).

The login form (class-login.php) explicitly adds wp_nonce_field('um_login_form') and verifies it — so the omission on the reset form is deliberate and inconsistent.

ONLY defense present: a honeypot hidden field (um_add_security_checks in um-actions-global.php:32-48). A honeypot is trivially bypassed by any scripted HTTP client that simply omits the honeypot field or sends it empty, which is exactly what a CSRF attack does (the victim's browser sends the form, not a bot, so the honeypot is always empty). reCAPTCHA is a separate optional extension that is disabled by default and not part of the core plugin.

IMPACT CORRECTION — the claim says the attacker can "UM()->user()->password_reset($data->ID) for whatever username_b value was submitted." This is correct, but password_reset() (class-user.php:1477) only enqueues a reset-link EMAIL to the target user — it does not change the password, invalidate sessions, or expose credentials. A successful CSRF only causes an unsolicited password-reset email to be sent to the victim. This is a real CSRF but the blast radius is: (1) email-inbox flooding of a target user, (2) minor UX disruption. Account takeover is NOT possible from this step alone because the attacker cannot intercept the emailed reset link.

Therefore: is_real=true, severity=low (not medium — no direct account compromise, only unwanted email trigger), auth_required=none, confidence=high.
